content.text: Hello buddy, welcome to the StarCar Repo. It's a fresh repo for us to work out of. Two initial taskings: Read all the documentation and once you've ingested that and executed any tasking required by it, proceed to the github project StarCar (id=6) and review the first issue (starcar#1) and proceed from there.
I'll start by mapping the repo and reading everything in it.

input.command: git log --oneline -10 && echo "---TREE---" && find . -type f -not -path './.git/*' | sort
input.description: List repo files and recent commits

1	# StarCar
2	
3	**A visualizer for multi-agent / subagent processing.** Trains, cars, gates, and freight:
4	watch an agentic development pipeline the way a dispatcher watches a rail yard.
5	
6	## Status
7	
8	Pre-design. This repository opens **process-first**: before any code lands, it carries the
9	governing documents that will build it — a constitution, a self-healing process loop, and a
10	seed of hard-won operating rules ported from months of running a heavily-gated multi-agent
11	development process on a production project.
12	
13	That ordering is deliberate, and it is the point. Plenty of dashboards exist. What this
14	repo demonstrates is the *discipline* the dashboard visualizes: every feature here will be
15	designed, adversarially reviewed, test-driven, and gated by the same process the visualizer
16	renders — with the review verdicts and REJECT records committed in-repo as they happen.
17	
18	## The idea
19	
20	An agentic dev shop starts to look like a freight operation:
21	
22	- **Tracks = trains**: units of work composed from a single manifest, run as a sequence of
23	  implementation "cars," each with its own adversarial reviewer.
24	- **Signals = gates**: design review, spec review, plan review, per-car review, the
25	  whole-branch gate, CI — each with binding authority to stop the line.
26	- **Inbound freight = the ticket queue**, grouped by area into future manifests.
27	- **The fuel gauge = the usage meter** the whole operation is budgeted against.
28	
29	StarCar renders that live, from pluggable data adapters (a git repo, an issue tracker's
30	project board, a conductor-maintained state file) — never hardcoded to any one shop.
31	
32	## Reading order
33	
34	1. [`docs/constitution.md`](docs/constitution.md) — what this project must be (DRAFT until
35	   ratified).
36	2. [`docs/the-healing-loop.md`](docs/the-healing-loop.md) — how the process that builds it
37	   repairs and hardens itself.
38	3. [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) — the operating rules, each carrying the scar that earned it.
39	4. `docs/templates/` — the working artifacts (car briefs, state ledger, gating matrix).
40	
41	## License
42	
43	MIT.
44

1	# The StarCar Constitution
2	
3	Status: **RATIFIED by the owner, 2026-07-21** - the founding day, before any code.
4	Derived from the meta-principles of a sibling project's constitution (eight ordered laws
5	governing a race-strategy system); the SHAPE ports, the content is re-derived for what
6	StarCar is: a visualizer that renders the truth of a multi-agent process to the human
7	running it. Ordered - earlier laws outrank later ones when they conflict. Amendments go
8	through the owner; every capability, fix, and review in this repo is judged against
9	these laws by name.
10	
11	## 1. Truth
12	
13	StarCar never renders what it cannot back. A yard board that shows a car as merged when
14	it is not, a gate as green when it is pending, or a train as idle when it is burning
15	tokens is worse than no board at all - a confident falsehood on a status surface is the
16	worst defect this project can ship, because the whole point of the project is that the
17	human trusts the board instead of asking. Unknown states render AS unknown, honestly.
18	
19	## 2. The Dispatcher Commands
20	
21	The human running the yard outranks the board. StarCar informs decisions; it never makes
22	them, never hides information "for their own good," and never resists an override. If a
23	dispatcher marks a train held, the board renders held - even if the data says rolling.
24	
25	## 3. Actionability
26	
27	The board exists to answer "what needs my attention NOW" faster than reading logs. Every
28	rendered element earns its pixels by shortening the path from state to decision: a
29	REJECT should be visible before the report is read; a stalled car should look stalled at
30	a glance. Decoration that does not inform is cut.
31	
32	## 4. Nothing Silently Lost
33	
34	Every train, car, verdict, and gate outcome the adapters can see is rendered or counted -
35	never silently dropped. If an adapter fails or a source is unreachable, the board says
36	so, loudly, in place: a missing lane reading as "no trains" is a lie of omission.
37	
38	## 5. Self-Knowledge
39	
40	StarCar is honest about its own state: data freshness is always visible (as-of
41	timestamps, staleness warnings), adapter health is a first-class surface, and the board
42	degrades loudly, never silently. A stale board that looks live is the lying-canary
43	disease this project's ancestors fought all year.
44	
45	## 6. One Truth
46	
47	Adapters own the facts; the view renders them. The UI never computes its own state,
48	never derives a verdict the source system did not issue, and never maintains a second
49	copy of anything that can drift. Where two sources disagree, the board SHOWS the
50	disagreement rather than picking a winner silently.
51	
52	## 7. The Stranger
53	
54	StarCar is built for the shop that did not build it: pluggable adapters, no hardcoded
55	board schemas or label taxonomies, synthetic demo data in-repo, and documentation a
56	stranger can deploy from. The flagship deployment is private; the project is public.
57	
58	## 8. Growth
59	
60	Every incident in StarCar's own development feeds the Healing Loop: classified to class,
61	guarded mechanically, written into the institution. The repo's review records are public
62	on purpose - the process is the showcase.
63

1	# The Healing Loop
2	
3	Companion to [the constitution](constitution.md). The constitution is what this project
4	must be; the Healing Loop is the metabolism - how the process that builds it repairs and
5	hardens itself. Ported from the shop that derived it, where it ran unassisted several
6	times in a single morning before anyone named it.
7	
8	## The observation that started it
9	
10	As an agentic codebase and its process mature, the consistent failure surface migrates UP
11	the stack: from syntax, to logic, to integration, to coordination and epistemics - who
12	knows what, who checked what, what a scope includes, where knowledge lives. The workers
13	barely miss; the misses live between the scopes. This is the journey human organizations
14	took over decades ("a bad system will beat a good person every time" - Deming),
15	compressed into weeks, with two twists unique to agentic development:
16	
17	1. **Every worker is a new hire on day one.** Agents accumulate no experience across
18	   dispatches. Only what is WRITTEN persists. Process here is not bureaucracy around the
19	   workforce; it IS the institution. The workforce evaporates nightly.
20	2. **The economics inverted.** A human org rations review because review labor is scarce.
21	   Here a skeptical expert costs one dispatch, so the constraint flips from "who can we
22	   afford to check this" to "have we DESIGNED a check for this failure class." The binding
23	   constraint is review attention, not generation - so the system optimizes for the least
24	   new-code-reviewed per unit of capability, and for gates that catch classes, not
25	   instances.
26	
27	## The loop
28	
29	Every failure, at every altitude (code, plan, spec, design, process, tooling,
30	instrument), gets the same four-step treatment:
31	
32	1. **Classify to the CLASS, not the instance.** "The DTO dropped nine fields" is an
33	   instance. "Hand-maintained mirrors at process boundaries fail silently, and no review
34	   scope owned the whole path" is the class. Root-cause analysis is not done until the
35	   class is named.
36	2. **Install a mechanical guard at the cheapest layer that could have caught it.** A test
37	   beats a review rule; a review rule beats a standing procedure; a procedure beats
38	   vigilance; a structural impossibility beats them all (an agent type that cannot
39	   delegate because the tool is absent, not because a rule forbids it). Prefer guards
40	   that fire at build/test time over guards that require someone to remember.
41	3. **Give the guard binding authority.** Advisory checks decay into wallpaper. Every
42	   adversarial gate holds REJECT; any Major = REJECT; disclosed-but-wrong does not clear
43	   review. Rejection appeals upward (author → conductor ruling → owner), never around.
44	4. **Write it into the institution.** Operating rules, ledgers, procedures, agent
45	   definitions, scenario files, corpus fixtures. The written system is the only system
46	   that survives the night.
47	
48	## The preconditions (why the loop works)
49	
50	- **Honest-failure framing.** Immune systems only work on true signals. Because an honest
51	  stop, a disclosed miss, and a reviewer's REJECT are all named SUCCESS outcomes, agents
52	  surface their own failures - workers disclose defects in their own diffs, reviewers
53	  reject their own train's plans. A blame-shaped system hides exactly the signals healing
54	  requires. This framing is the load-bearing wall.
55	- **Adversaries at every altitude.** Design review, spec review, plan review, per-car
56	  review with the sentence check, whole-branch gate, CI, production. Each catches a
57	  different failure class at the cheapest point that class is catchable. Each gate should
58	  be installed by the loop itself, one incident at a time - imported ceremony that never
59	  caught anything here is a pruning candidate.
60	- **Executable knowledge.** Validated facts must land as tests or gates, never only
61	  prose. Prose knowledge defends nothing - the team that learned it is gone by morning.
62	- **Instruments inside the loop.** The harnesses that judge the product are audited
63	  against the product's own changes on a cadence, with a severity philosophy that keeps
64	  them honest: expected patterns are notes, defects are flags, and an instrument that
65	  cries wolf is worse than no instrument.
66	- **One doctrine, independently re-derivable.** The test that the loop is working: two
67	  reviewers at different gates deriving the same principle from different incidents,
68	  without coordination. When the system's parts converge on consistent doctrine
69	  unprompted, the doctrine is real.
70	
71	## The honest edges
72	
73	- **Nothing prunes by default.** Immune systems that only add gates go autoimmune:
74	  ritual, token cost, friction without catches. The counter-organ is the scorecard -
75	  catches per gate, reviewed on a cadence; a gate that stops catching gets retired with
76	  the same deliberateness it was installed. Young projects importing this seed should
77	  watch this edge hardest: start with the right-sized subset, let incidents install the
78	  rest.
79	- **The loop is human-supervised, and that is a design choice, not a gap.** "Stop the
80	  train" is a human immune response no gate is watching for; the arbiter above every gate
81	  is the owner. The standing design question, revisited as the loop matures: which owner
82	  interventions should become gates, and which are permanently, properly human.
83	- **Rules bind only where they are structural.** A prose rule binds an agent that reads
84	  it; a toolset binds every agent regardless. Over time, rules that matter should
85	  graduate from prose to structure. The gap between what is written and what is enforced
86	  is itself a failure surface the loop should keep eating.
87

1	# Tooling setup - what is installed, what installs later, and what triggers it
2	
3	Status: Current
4	
5	An agent opening this repo on the founding box starts with everything below in the
6	"ready now" state. Items in "installs later" have explicit triggers - install them WHEN
7	the trigger fires, not before (right-sizing: tooling earns its place like everything
8	else).
9	
10	## Ready now (committed in-repo)
11	
12	| Thing | Where | Notes |
13	|---|---|---|
14	| Process seed | `CLAUDE.md` | The operating rules, scars attached |
15	| Constitution | `docs/constitution.md` | RATIFIED 2026-07-21, before any code |
16	| Healing Loop | `docs/the-healing-loop.md` | The process metabolism |
17	| `car` agent type | `.claude/agents/car.md` | No-delegation implementer/reviewer (toolset-enforced) |
18	| `/goodnight` skill | `.claude/skills/goodnight/` | Session-end ritual + resume packets |
19	| Resume-packet hook | `.claude/hooks/goodnight-resume-check.sh` | SessionStart announce, wired in `.claude/settings.json` |
20	| Board tooling | `scripts/board.ps1` + `Board.psm1` (+ Pester tests) | Defaults: user project 6, repo polecatspeaks/StarCar; pure logic Pester-tested |
21	| Templates | `docs/templates/` | Car brief, state ledger, gating matrix |
22	| MCP: context7 | `.mcp.json` | Library docs lookup, stack-agnostic |
23	| MCP: gitnexus | `.mcp.json` | Server registered; serves nothing until first index (below) |
24	| pr-review-toolkit plugin | `.claude/settings.json` | Enabled |
25	
26	Also available from the box/user level (nothing to install per-repo): the superpowers
27	plugin (brainstorming, writing-plans, TDD skills), the Claude-in-Chrome browser tools.
28	
29	## Installs later - trigger-gated
30	
31	| Thing | Trigger | How |
32	|---|---|---|
33	| GitNexus index | FIRST CODE lands (nothing to index before that) | `npx gitnexus analyze` (or `npm i -g gitnexus` then `gitnexus analyze`); rerun with `--pdg` once the codebase is worth statement-level analysis. The MCP server in `.mcp.json` starts serving as soon as the index exists. |
34	| CI + repo policy | First workflow need (likely: first code PR) | Port the docs `Status:` line checker + a build/test workflow; keep the "verified means CI went green" rule from CLAUDE.md in force from day one |
35	| Session-start env hook | The repo is opened in a web/container environment | Port the ancestor's `session-start.sh` PATTERN (idempotent installs, strictly non-fatal guards, wrapper-on-PATH) with THIS stack's tools - the ancestor's dotnet/pwsh specifics do not apply |
36	| Suite runner / CI watcher scripts | First test suite / first CI | Generalize from the ancestor shop's `run-suites` / `watch-ci` patterns when there is something to run |
37	
38	## Entire.io session mirroring: ENABLED (owner decision, 2026-07-21)
39	
40	Every agent-session transcript in this repo publishes to the public checkpoint branch -
41	by design ("the full monty"): the development process is the showcase, radically
42	transparent and verifiably real, half-thoughts included. The price of that is the HARD
43	INVARIANT at the top of CLAUDE.md: StarCar sessions are StarCar-only; nothing from any
44	other project is ever typed here. `entire enable` + `entire agent add claude-code` are
45	done (hooks in `.claude/settings.json`, config in `.entire/`); `entire search` is the
46	entry point for decision archaeology.
47

1	# Car brief template
2	
3	Every dispatch uses this shape. The framing rules are load-bearing: agents gradient
4	toward success shapes, so truth-telling must BE a success shape.
5	
6	```
7	You are Car <ID> on the <train> (repo: <repo>, <one-line project context>). You implement
8	<exact task list> from <the plan doc>. Work ONLY in the worktree at <path> on branch
9	<branch>. NEVER touch the shared checkout at <path>. Commit locally per task; NEVER push.
10	NO NESTED DELEGATION. <Platform notes: which suites run here, how.>
11	
12	FIRST verify base: `git log -1 --format='%H'` must show <sha> and the branch must be
13	<branch>. If not, STOP and report - do not proceed on a stale base.
14	
15	YOUR ORDERS: <plan doc path> - read <sections> IN FULL and execute in order, red-first
16	(write the failing test, RUN it, confirm it fails FOR THE STATED REASON - a red failing
17	for a different reason is a finding to report, not paper over). Ground truth on
18	ambiguity: <spec doc path>. Execute ONLY your tasks - <other cars' scopes> belong to
19	later cars; do not touch their files.
20	
21	<CROSS-CAR CONTEXT: what is already merged in the base, amendments that supersede stale
22	plan text, warnings from earlier cars' reviewers - verbatim, with file:line.>
23	
24	STANDING RULES binding every commit:
25	- <state ledger / contracts rule with current baseline numbers and the STOP-if-different
26	  instruction>
27	- <lifecycle-test rule for new mutable state>
28	- The plan was verified against real code at <sha>, but if ANY snippet fails to compile
29	  or names a missing API, that is a plan defect: HONEST-STOP on that task with the exact
30	  error and file:line, continue with independent tasks. Honest stops are SUCCESS
31	  outcomes; improvising past a contradiction is the failure mode that costs trains.
32	- After your last task run <the full suite list with expected baselines> and build clean.
33	
34	FINAL REPORT: per task - commit SHA, red evidence (test name + observed failure reason),
35	green evidence (counts), deviations with justification; then total suite results, ledger
36	arithmetic as committed, findings/disclosures/honest stops. Your report feeds your
37	adversarial reviewer - make every claim verifiable.
38	```
39	
40	## Reviewer brief addendum
41	
42	```
43	You are the adversarial sentence-check reviewer for Car <ID>. Binding REJECT authority:
44	any Major = REJECT; disclosed-but-wrong does not clear review. READ-ONLY: edit nothing,
45	commit nothing, push nothing. Work ONLY in the detached worktree at <path> (HEAD must be
46	<sha> - verify first, STOP if not).
47	
48	SCOPE: <commits> against <plan/spec sections>.
49	VERIFY: <the specific claims from the car's report, each with how to check it>.
50	THE SENTENCE CHECK: <any cross-boundary value in this diff> - trace producer to final
51	consumer, every hop file:line, every hand-maintained mirror checked.
52	ADJUDICATIONS: <each disclosed deviation, to be ruled on against real code>.
53	RUN YOURSELF: <suites + expected counts>. Report observed.
54	CONSTITUTION CHECK: name each law the diff implicates, one line of evidence each.
55	
56	VERDICT: APPROVE or REJECT up top; findings by severity with file:line.
57	```
58

1	# Gating matrix (template)
2	
3	Status: template - copy to `docs/contracts/gating-matrix.md` when the first gated
4	surface lands.
5	
6	The living inventory of every surface that DECIDES something (fires an alert, suppresses
7	an output, flips a status, blocks an action): what gates it, what resets the gate, and
8	the evidence. Any commit touching gating updates this file in the same commit.
9	
10	Why: gates drift from the code they guard, and a stale gate description is a lying
11	canary - the reader trusts the documented mechanism while the code does something else.
12	The ancestor project's reviews caught mechanism-prose drift within hours of a mechanism
13	change; that is the standard.
14	
15	## Header (keep count current)
16	
17	N surfaces audited.
18	
19	## Table shape
20	
21	| Surface | Fires when | Suppressed when | Resets on | Classification | Evidence (test name) |
22	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
23	| Example: staleness banner | data age > threshold | never (truth surface) | fresh fetch | DELIBERATE, no override | `Banner_Stale_...` |
24	
25	Severity philosophy for anything that judges data: expected/placeholder patterns are
26	NOTES, defects are FLAGS. An instrument that cries wolf is worse than no instrument.
27

1	# State ledger (template)
2	
3	Status: template - copy to `docs/contracts/state-ledger.md` when the first mutable
4	service state lands.
5	
6	The living inventory of every mutable field in long-lived services, with a verdict per
7	lifecycle event. Any commit adding or changing such state updates this file IN THE SAME
8	COMMIT, with old → delta → new arithmetic in the header. Reviewers reject state-touching
9	diffs that leave this file stale.
10	
11	Why: state that silently survives (or silently resets across) a lifecycle event is the
12	highest-yield latent-bug class in long-lived agentic systems - an audit of this
13	template's ancestor project found 99 latent instances of the same pattern, each
14	individually "too small to ledger."
15	
16	## Header (keep arithmetic current)
17	
18	Verdict totals across N fields: SAFE n, LATENT_BUG n, DELIBERATE_CARRY n.
19	
20	## Lifecycle events (enumerate YOURS - these are examples)
21	
22	Define the project's lifecycle events once, then verdict every field against each:
23	process restart | data-source reconnect | adapter failure window | config reload
24	
25	## Table shape
26	
27	| Field (owner class) | Event 1 | Event 2 | Event 3 | Event 4 | Verdict | Evidence (test name) |
28	|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
29	| `_example` (ExampleService) | RESET | CARRY | FREEZE | LOST (honest) | SAFE | `LifecycleEvent_Example_...` |
30	
31	Every row cites the red-first lifecycle tests that pin it. A row without tests is
32	LATENT_BUG until proven otherwise.
33

1	---
2	name: car
3	description: Implementer car / adversarial reviewer. Executes one brief directly in an isolated worktree. Structurally cannot spawn subagents - the dispatch tool is absent from its toolset by design (a prose rule binds an agent that reads it; a toolset binds every agent regardless). Use for every implementer dispatch and every adversarial reviewer dispatch; the conductor names the model explicitly on every dispatch.
4	tools: Bash, PowerShell, Read, Edit, Write, Glob, Grep, ToolSearch, TaskOutput
5	---
6	
7	You are a car: a single-purpose worker executing exactly one brief.
8	
9	- FIRST verify your base: the brief names a worktree, branch, and base commit. Check all
10	  three before any edit. If the worktree is on different history, STOP and report - do
11	  not proceed on a stale base.
12	- Work ONLY in your assigned worktree. Never touch the shared checkout.
13	- Commit locally. NEVER push. The conductor merges.
14	- Red-first TDD for every behavior change: write the failing test, RUN it, confirm it
15	  fails for the stated reason, then implement, then green, then state pass counts.
16	- If the brief contradicts the real code, HONEST-STOP on that item with file:line
17	  evidence and continue with independent work. An honest stop is a SUCCESS outcome; an
18	  improvised workaround is the failure mode that costs trains.
19	- Your final report is input to an adversarial reviewer: make every claim verifiable
20	  (commit SHAs, observed test failures verbatim, exact pass counts, file:line citations).
21	
22	As a REVIEWER you additionally: hold binding REJECT authority (any Major = REJECT;
23	disclosed-but-wrong does not clear); run the suites yourself rather than trusting the
24	report; trace any value crossing a process/serialization boundary hop-for-hop with
25	file:line (the sentence check); and end with a constitution check - name each law the
26	diff implicates with one line of evidence it is honored, or a finding where it is not.
27	You edit NOTHING, commit NOTHING, push NOTHING.
28

1	---
2	name: goodnight
3	description: Session-end ritual - triage in-flight work (wait or write a resume packet), push sweep, state checkpoint, board pass, CI disposition, and the yard-status close. Invoke when the owner signs off for the day or asks to close the session cleanly.
4	---
5	
6	# /goodnight - closing the yard
7	
8	A session ending is a DECISION POINT, not an event: background agents die with the
9	session, unpushed commits produce stale builds tomorrow, and unrecorded state costs the
10	morning session an archaeology dig. Run the steps in order.
11	
12	## 1. In-flight triage (FIRST)
13	
14	For every background agent still running: **wait** (if its car-and-review unit is close -
15	never strand one without the other) or **write a resume packet** to the project's memory
16	location, per item: branch, tip commit, worktree path, what it was doing, and what its
17	re-dispatch brief must say. Background agents DO NOT survive the session - the packet is
18	a re-dispatch spec, not a bookmark. Wire a SessionStart hook that announces a leftover
19	packet at the next boot; the resuming session reads it FIRST, re-dispatches, then deletes
20	it.
21	
22	## 2. Push sweep
23	
24	Shared checkout clean; default branch pushed (release pipelines build from the REMOTE).
25	Car worktree branches are exempt - they are recorded in the packet instead.
26	
27	## 3. State checkpoint (never skipped)
28	
29	Update the working-state memory to a literal RESUME HERE shape: what landed (merge SHAs),
30	what is parked and why, the exact next step. The written system is the only system that
31	survives the night.
32	
33	## 4. Board pass
34	
35	Issue tracker synced to reality; new tickets have a status.
36	
37	## 5. CI disposition
38	
39	Green, or pending-with-note. Red-and-deterministic is NOT a goodnight - it is a blocker
40	to surface before closing.
41	
42	## 6. The yard-status close
43	
44	Three sentences, written to memory AND said to the owner: what landed, what is parked,
45	what happens first tomorrow.
46	
47	## 7. Weekly only: worktree prune
48	
49	Remove worktrees whose branches are fully merged; never prune anything named in the
50	resume packet or holding uncommitted work.
51	
52	Rules: steps 1-3 never skipped; an honest "step N not done because X" beats a silent
53	skip; for a surprise ending ("gotta go NOW"): packet + checkpoint only, say what was
54	skipped.
55

1	# board.ps1 -- kanban ops by ISSUE NUMBER against GitHub Project 5 (#349 item 3).
2	#
3	# Wraps the manual `gh project` + GraphQL dance done by hand during the
4	# 2026-07-12 board passes (status moves, adds, top-of-column and after-item
5	# reordering). The project id, the Status field id, and its option ids are
6	# resolved at runtime every call via `gh project view` / `gh project
7	# field-list` -- never hardcoded, because they drifted once already.
8	#
9	# Pure parsing/lookup logic lives in Board.psm1 (Pester-tested with fixture
10	# JSON, no rig/token needed). This file is the thin gh wrapper: per the repo's
11	# TDD policy for CLI wrappers it is smoke-tested + documented rather than
12	# unit-tested (list + one reversible status round-trip on a closed Done issue;
13	# add/top/after are documented but not exercised against the live board -- see
14	# task-c2-report.md).
15	#
16	# Usage:
17	#   scripts/board.ps1 list [StatusName]
18	#   scripts/board.ps1 status <IssueNumber> <StatusName>
19	#   scripts/board.ps1 add <IssueNumber> [StatusName]
20	#   scripts/board.ps1 top <IssueNumber>
21	#   scripts/board.ps1 after <IssueNumber> <OtherIssueNumber>
22	#   scripts/board.ps1 status-batch <n1,n2,...> <StatusName>
23	#
24	# StatusName is validated against the LIVE field options (Backlog, Todo,
25	# In Progress, In Review, Done today, but never assumed).
26	#
27	# status-batch (#555): resolves project id + Status field + the item list
28	# ONCE, then applies N item-edits, instead of the per-call `status` command's
29	# full re-resolution each time (that pattern drained a whole GraphQL hour on
30	# ~20 operations on 2026-07-21). A single issue number missing from the board
31	# is reported and skipped, not fatal to the rest of the batch -- see
32	# Board.psm1's Resolve-BoardBatchItems (Pester-tested). An INDIVIDUAL gh
33	# item-edit failure mid-batch is likewise warned and skipped (the remaining
34	# edits still run; exit code 1 if anything missed or failed, 0 only on a
35	# fully clean batch) -- review 2026-07-21 asked for this to live here, not
36	# only in the commit message. Like the other write
37	# commands, this gh-wrapper loop is documented here rather than unit-tested
38	# (see task-c2-report.md precedent); only the parsing/lookup it calls into is
39	# unit-tested.
40	#
41	# Windows PowerShell 5.1 compatible: no ternary, no &&/||, ASCII only.
42	
43	param(
44	    [Parameter(Mandatory, Position = 0)]
45	    [ValidateSet('list', 'status', 'add', 'top', 'after', 'status-batch')]
46	    [string]$Command,
47	
48	    [Parameter(Position = 1)] [string]$Arg1,
49	    [Parameter(Position = 2)] [string]$Arg2,
50	
51	    [string]$Owner = 'polecatspeaks',
52	    [int]$ProjectNumber = 6,
53	    [string]$Repo = 'polecatspeaks/StarCar'
54	)
55	
56	Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
57	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
58	
59	Import-Module (Join-Path $PSScriptRoot 'Board.psm1') -Force
60	
61	function Get-ProjectIdLive {
62	    $raw = gh project view $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner --format json
63	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { throw "gh project view $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)." }
64	    ($raw | ConvertFrom-Json).id
65	}
66	
67	function Get-StatusFieldLive {
68	    # --limit: gh defaults to 30 fields; same drift rationale as item-list's --limit 500 (review 2026-07-12)
69	    $raw = gh project field-list $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner --format json --limit 100
70	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { throw "gh project field-list $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)." }
71	    Get-BoardStatusField -FieldsResult ($raw | ConvertFrom-Json)
72	}
73	
74	function Get-ItemsLive {
75	    # --limit 500: the project already has 200+ items (#349 ask: item-id
76	    # lookup must handle >200, well past gh's 30-item default).
77	    $raw = gh project item-list $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner --format json --limit 500
78	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { throw "gh project item-list $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)." }
79	    $raw | ConvertFrom-Json
80	}
81	
82	function Get-RequiredItem {
83	    param([Parameter(Mandatory)] [object]$Items, [Parameter(Mandatory)] [int]$IssueNumber)
84	
85	    $item = Find-BoardItemForIssue -ItemsResult $Items -IssueNumber $IssueNumber
86	    if (-not $item) {
87	        Write-Error "Issue #$IssueNumber is not on project $ProjectNumber. Use 'add' first."
88	        exit 1
89	    }
90	    return $item
91	}
92	
93	switch ($Command) {
94	    'list' {
95	        $items = Get-ItemsLive
96	        $filterStatus = $Arg1
97	        foreach ($item in $items.items) {
98	            if ($filterStatus -and $item.status -ne $filterStatus) { continue }
99	            Write-Host (Format-BoardListLine -Item $item)
100	        }
101	        exit 0
102	    }
103	
104	    'status' {
105	        if (-not $Arg1 -or -not $Arg2) {
106	            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 status <IssueNumber> <StatusName>'
107	            exit 2
108	        }
109	        $issueNumber = [int]$Arg1
110	        $statusName = $Arg2
111	
112	        $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
113	        $statusField = Get-StatusFieldLive
114	        $optionId = Resolve-BoardStatusOptionId -StatusField $statusField -StatusName $statusName
115	
116	        $items = Get-ItemsLive
117	        $item = Get-RequiredItem -Items $items -IssueNumber $issueNumber
118	
119	        gh project item-edit --id $item.ItemId --field-id $statusField.FieldId --project-id $projectId --single-select-option-id $optionId | Out-Null
120	        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh project item-edit failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }
121	
122	        Write-Host "#$issueNumber ($($item.Title)) -> $statusName"
123	        exit 0
124	    }
125	
126	    'add' {
127	        if (-not $Arg1) {
128	            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 add <IssueNumber> [StatusName]'
129	            exit 2
130	        }
131	        $issueNumber = [int]$Arg1
132	        $url = "https://github.com/$Repo/issues/$issueNumber"
133	
134	        $raw = gh project item-add $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner --url $url --format json
135	        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh project item-add failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }
136	        $added = $raw | ConvertFrom-Json
137	        Write-Host "#$issueNumber added to project $ProjectNumber (item id $($added.id))."
138	
139	        if ($Arg2) {
140	            $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
141	            $statusField = Get-StatusFieldLive
142	            $optionId = Resolve-BoardStatusOptionId -StatusField $statusField -StatusName $Arg2
143	            gh project item-edit --id $added.id --field-id $statusField.FieldId --project-id $projectId --single-select-option-id $optionId | Out-Null
144	            if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh project item-edit failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }
145	            Write-Host "#$issueNumber -> $Arg2"
146	        }
147	        exit 0
148	    }
149	
150	    'top' {
151	        if (-not $Arg1) {
152	            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 top <IssueNumber>'
153	            exit 2
154	        }
155	        $issueNumber = [int]$Arg1
156	        $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
157	        $items = Get-ItemsLive
158	        $item = Get-RequiredItem -Items $items -IssueNumber $issueNumber
159	
160	        # Omitting afterId (not passing null/empty) is what moves the item to
161	        # the very top -- UpdateProjectV2ItemPositionInput.afterId is a
162	        # nullable, optional ID (confirmed via GraphQL schema introspection).
163	        $query = 'mutation($projectId: ID!, $itemId: ID!) { updateProjectV2ItemPosition(input: {projectId: $projectId, itemId: $itemId}) { clientMutationId } }'
164	        gh api graphql -f query=$query -f "projectId=$projectId" -f "itemId=$($item.ItemId)" | Out-Null
165	        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh api graphql (updateProjectV2ItemPosition) failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }
166	
167	        Write-Host "#$issueNumber moved to top."
168	        exit 0
169	    }
170	
171	    'after' {
172	        if (-not $Arg1 -or -not $Arg2) {
173	            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 after <IssueNumber> <OtherIssueNumber>'
174	            exit 2
175	        }
176	        $issueNumber = [int]$Arg1
177	        $otherIssueNumber = [int]$Arg2
178	        $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
179	        $items = Get-ItemsLive
180	        $item = Get-RequiredItem -Items $items -IssueNumber $issueNumber
181	        $otherItem = Get-RequiredItem -Items $items -IssueNumber $otherIssueNumber
182	
183	        $query = 'mutation($projectId: ID!, $itemId: ID!, $afterId: ID!) { updateProjectV2ItemPosition(input: {projectId: $projectId, itemId: $itemId, afterId: $afterId}) { clientMutationId } }'
184	        gh api graphql -f query=$query -f "projectId=$projectId" -f "itemId=$($item.ItemId)" -f "afterId=$($otherItem.ItemId)" | Out-Null
185	        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh api graphql (updateProjectV2ItemPosition) failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }
186	
187	        Write-Host "#$issueNumber moved after #$otherIssueNumber."
188	        exit 0
189	    }
190	
191	    'status-batch' {
192	        if (-not $Arg1 -or -not $Arg2) {
193	            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 status-batch <n1,n2,...> <StatusName>'
194	            exit 2
195	        }
196	        $statusName = $Arg2
197	        $issueNumbers = ConvertFrom-BoardIssueNumberList -IssueNumbersText $Arg1
198	
199	        # Resolve metadata ONCE (#555): this is the whole point of the batch
200	        # command over N `status` calls, each of which re-resolves all three.
201	        $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
202	        $statusField = Get-StatusFieldLive
203	        $optionId = Resolve-BoardStatusOptionId -StatusField $statusField -StatusName $statusName
204	        $items = Get-ItemsLive
205	
206	        $resolution = Resolve-BoardBatchItems -ItemsResult $items -IssueNumbers $issueNumbers
207	
208	        foreach ($missingIssue in $resolution.Missing) {
209	            Write-Warning "#$missingIssue is not on project $ProjectNumber. Skipped (use 'add' first)."
210	        }
211	
212	        $editFailures = @()
213	        foreach ($found in $resolution.Found) {
214	            gh project item-edit --id $found.ItemId --field-id $statusField.FieldId --project-id $projectId --single-select-option-id $optionId | Out-Null
215	            if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) {
216	                Write-Warning "#$($found.IssueNumber) ($($found.Title)): gh project item-edit failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE). Skipped, continuing batch."
217	                $editFailures += $found.IssueNumber
218	                continue
219	            }
220	            Write-Host "#$($found.IssueNumber) ($($found.Title)) -> $statusName"
221	        }
222	
223	        $editedCount = $resolution.Found.Count - $editFailures.Count
224	        Write-Host "status-batch: $editedCount edited, $($resolution.Missing.Count) missing, $($editFailures.Count) edit failure(s)."
225	
226	        if ($resolution.Missing.Count -gt 0 -or $editFailures.Count -gt 0) { exit 1 }
227	        exit 0
228	    }
229	}
230

1	#!/bin/sh
2	# SessionStart hook: announce a /goodnight resume packet if one exists.
3	# Written by the goodnight skill (.claude/skills/goodnight/SKILL.md) when a session
4	# closes with agents still in flight. Background agents die with their session, so the
5	# packet is a re-dispatch spec the next session must read FIRST.
6	packet="$HOME/.claude/projects/C--Users-Chris-git-starcar/memory/resume-packet.md"
7	if [ -f "$packet" ]; then
8	  echo "[goodnight] RESUME PACKET FOUND from the previous session: $packet"
9	  echo "[goodnight] Unfinished work is recorded there. Read it FIRST, fold it into the"
10	  echo "[goodnight] working-state memory, re-dispatch what it names, then DELETE the packet."
11	fi
12	exit 0
13

DATA: pluggable adapters only (a git repo, an issue tracker's project board, a conductor-maintained state JSON) - never hardcoded to any one shop (Law 7, The Stranger); data freshness always visible (Law 5); adapters own facts, the view never computes its own (Law 6). ROUTE: v0 static mock first to validate the layout, then the adapter/generator built through the full ladder (this repo's CLAUDE.md), with review verdicts committed in-repo as they happen - the process is the showcase. Pacing: owner-paced side project. content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\Chris\.claude\plugins\cache\claude-plugins-official\superpowers\6.1.1\skills\brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer the visual companion just-in-time** — NOT upfront. The first time a question would genuinely be clearer shown than described, offer it then (its own message); on approval its browser tab opens for you. If no visual question ever arises, never offer it. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion (just-in-time):** Do NOT offer it upfront. Wait until a question would genuinely be clearer shown than told — a real mockup / layout / diagram question, not merely a UI *topic*. The first time that happens, offer it then, as its own message:
> "This next part might be easier if I show you — I can put together mockups, diagrams, and comparisons in a browser tab as we go. It's still new and can be token-intensive. Want me to? I'll open it for you."

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Only the offer — no clarifying question, summary, or other content. Wait for the user's response. If they accept, start the server with `--open` so their browser opens to the first screen automatically. If they decline, continue text-only and don't offer again unless they raise it.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`


ARGUMENTS: StarCar issue #1 - the yard visualizer v0: turn the founding-day design sketch into a reviewable design doc

Docs ingested — all of it: `README.md`, `docs/constitution.md` (8 laws, ratified today), `docs/the-healing-loop.md`, `CLAUDE.md` (the process seed + the HARD INVARIANT), `docs/setup.md`, the three templates, `.claude/agents/car.md`, the `/goodnight` skill, `.mcp.json`, `.claude/settings.json`, and the board scripts.

**Tasking executed from the docs:**
- SessionStart resume-packet hook fired and announced nothing → no work parked from a prior session. Clean start.
- `docs/setup.md` trigger-gated installs: GitNexus index (trigger = *first code lands*), CI (trigger = *first workflow need*), session-start env hook (trigger = *web/container env*), suite runner (trigger = *first test suite*). **None of those triggers have fired.** Right-sizing rule says install when the trigger fires, not before — so I installed nothing.
- HARD INVARIANT acknowledged: this session is StarCar-only. Nothing from any other project gets typed here.

**Two findings from the read (both small, both reportable):**

1. **`scripts/board.ps1:1` lies about what it targets.** The header comment says *"against GitHub Project 5 (#349 item 3)"* and cites `task-c2-report.md` and issues #349/#555 — all ancestor-shop artifacts that don't exist in this repo. The actual defaults (`board.ps1:52-53`) are project **6**, `polecatspeaks/StarCar`. Constitution Law 1 turned on our own tooling: a comment describing a different project is exactly the lying-canary disease. Cheap fix, but it should be a ticket, not a silent edit.

2. **Issue #1 has no label, and no area taxonomy exists.** `CLAUDE.md` Tracking says *one area label per issue* and *a train is composed from ONE label's tickets*. The repo carries only GitHub's stock labels (bug/enhancement/documentation/…) — there is no area vocabulary yet. With one issue it costs nothing; at ten issues it costs a manifest.

---

**On issue #1 itself:** it's the founding epic, not a task — `The yard visualizer: tracks, signals, freight, and fuel`, Todo on project 6, sole item on the board. It already carries a design sketch from the founding-day brainstorm (four surfaces, pluggable adapters, Laws 5/6/7 cited) and names the route: *v0 static mock first to validate the layout, then the adapter/generator through the full ladder*.

So the right next move is to turn that sketch into a reviewable design doc — which is the ladder's first rung. I'm using the brainstorming skill to do that, and I need a handful of decisions from you first. One at a time.

Starting with the one that constrains everything downstream:

questions.question: What is StarCar, as a thing that runs? The founding sketch fixes the content (trains/gates/freight/fuel) but not the medium, and the medium constrains the mock, the adapters, the test strategy, and what "a stranger can deploy this" (Law 7) even means.

options.label: Static-generating web board (Recommended)
options.description: A generator reads adapters and emits a self-contained HTML page; you open it in a browser, refresh to re-poll. No server, no runtime deps for the viewer. Cheapest v0 (the mock IS the target format, so the mock is not throwaway), trivially publishable as the public showcase, and a stranger deploys it by running one command. Zero inference means zero Law 1 risk, and the file doubles as the in-repo synthetic demo data Law 7 asks for. Cost: it is bookkeeping, and a conductor who forgets to write makes a stale board.

content.text:
Four decisions locked. That supersedes issue #1's "static mock first" route, so **the issue needs an amendment comment recording the change and why** (the docs' own rule: rulings get recorded, not silently applied). I'll do that once you approve the design.

Here's the design.

---

## StarCar v0 — the walking skeleton

### Architecture (six units, each independently testable)

**1. `Adapter<T>` — the contract.** One method, `poll(): Promise<AdapterResult<T>>`, which returns *either* data with an `asOf` stamp *or* an explicit failure with a human-readable reason. **It never throws into the view and health is part of the return value, not a side channel.** Law 5 makes adapter health a first-class surface; putting it in the return type means a lane physically cannot render without carrying its own health.

**2. `StateFileAdapter`** — reads `state.json`, validates it against a schema, returns a `TrainsSnapshot` or an `AdapterFailure` naming the validation error.

**3. `SnapshotAssembler`** — combines adapter results into a `YardSnapshot`. This is where Law 4 stops being prose and becomes a type:

```ts
type LaneState<T> =
  | { kind: 'ok';         data: T; asOf: string }
  | { kind: 'stale';      data: T; asOf: string; ageMs: number }
  | { kind: 'failed';     reason: string; lastGood?: T }
  | { kind: 'no-adapter' }

type YardSnapshot = {
  asOf: string
  lanes: { trains: LaneState<Train[]>; signals: LaneState<Gate[]>;
           freight: LaneState<Ticket[]>; fuel: LaneState<Fuel> }
}
```

`lanes` has four **required** keys and every value is a discriminated union. There is no way to express "this lane is absent" — the compiler will not let you omit a lane or hand back a bare array. *A lie of omission becomes a type error.* That's the Healing Loop's step-2 preference: a structural impossibility beats a review rule.

**4. `Server`** — serves the view assets, `GET /api/snapshot` (first paint), `GET /api/stream` (SSE). Poll loop on an interval; pushes only when the snapshot changes.

**5. `View`** — plain TS + DOM, no framework. Pure `render(snapshot) → DOM`, so it tests under jsdom without a browser.

**6. `Staleness`** — deliberately *two* independent truths, never merged: the **server** knows "my last successful poll was N seconds ago" (data stale); the **client** knows "my SSE pipe is down" (connection lost). These fail independently and a board that collapses them into one indicator will lie in exactly one of the two cases. Law 6: where two sources disagree, show the disagreement.

### Data flow

```
state.json --poll--> StateFileAdapter --> SnapshotAssembler --> /api/snapshot ---> View --> DOM
                                                            \-> /api/stream (SSE) -/
```

### Error handling

| Failure | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Adapter throws | Caught at boundary → `{kind:'failed', reason}` → red panel showing the reason. Never a blank lane. |
| Malformed `state.json` | Validation error → `failed` lane with the error text. **No partial render** (Law 1: never render what you can't back). |
| File missing / unreadable | `failed` with the OS reason. |
| SSE disconnect | Client shows "disconnected, last good as-of X", reconnects with backoff. Data stays on screen but *visibly* marked. |
| Lane has no adapter yet | `no-adapter` panel, loud. |

### Testing (all red-first)

Fixture corpus of `state.json` variants (valid / malformed / missing / partial) drives the adapter tests. The load-bearing ones: **a test asserting an unimplemented lane renders `no-adapter` and not empty** (Law 4 pinned mechanically), and **a fake-clock staleness test** crossing the threshold.

This train lands the first mutable service state — `lastGoodSnapshot`, `lastPollAt`, `connectedClients` — so **`docs/contracts/state-ledger.md` gets instantiated in the same commit**, with StarCar's lifecycle events enumerated for the first time: *server restart · adapter failure window · client reconnect · state-file replaced-or-truncated*. Each field gets a red-first verdict against all four.

### Explicitly NOT in this train (YAGNI)

No git adapter. No board adapter. No fuel adapter. No auth. No persistence. No framework. No CLI for writing `state.json` (hand-written and git-tracked at v0). No theming beyond one legible look.

### Cost line (CLAUDE.md cost discipline — approve before dispatch)

Full ladder is ~11 dispatches: design review 1, spec 1 + review 1, plan 1 + review 1, then ~3 cars each with its own reviewer. A right-sized alternative folds design and spec into one document (at this size the design *is* the spec), saving 2.

---

questions.question: Does this design hold, and how much ladder does it get? Cost discipline says the budget owner approves spend along with scope, before dispatch.

options.label: Approved - fold design+spec (Recommended)
options.description: Design holds. Write it as one design/spec document, send it to ONE adversarial design review, then plan -> plan review -> cars. ~9 dispatches. Justification: the design already fixes the data contract to type-level precision, so a separate spec document would mostly restate it - and CLAUDE.md warns that maximum ceremony on every change is itself a process failure. The design review still holds full REJECT authority.

content.text:
Board synced — and it surfaced a third finding immediately.

**Finding 3: project 6 can't represent the process it feeds.** Its Status field offers only `Todo / In Progress / Done`. The ladder needs **In Review** as a real state (a car sitting at inspection awaiting its adversarial reviewer is neither in progress nor done — and "a car at inspection looks in-progress" is precisely the Law 1 falsehood StarCar exists to kill). Issue #1's sketch also names a **holding yard** for deferred work, which wants Backlog. The board is a planned adapter source, so its columns being narrower than the process is a data-truth problem, not a cosmetic one. `board.ps1`'s live-option validation is what caught it — the script refusing to accept `Backlog` rather than guessing is the tooling behaving exactly as designed.

Filed so far: **#2** (`area:tooling`), **#3** (`area:process`), both Todo on the board. #1 now labeled `area:view`.

Now, back to the design — I need to know what to fix:

questions.question: Which part of the v0 design needs rework? Pick as many as apply, or use Other to say it in your own words - if the objection is something I did not anticipate, that is more useful than picking the closest box.

options.label: Unit boundaries / LaneState contract
options.description: The six-unit split (Adapter, StateFileAdapter, SnapshotAssembler, Server, View, Staleness) or the LaneState discriminated union that makes a missing lane a compile error. This is the load-bearing part - every later adapter inherits this contract, so if it is wrong it is worth catching now.

options.label: Staleness / error-handling behavior
options.description: The call to keep server-side data-staleness and client-side connection-loss as two independent, separately-rendered truths rather than one indicator. Or anything in the error-handling table: the no-partial-render rule on malformed input, keeping last-good data on screen during a disconnect, what a failed lane shows.

TWO truths, never merged:
  server: 'last good poll was N sec ago'
  client: 'my SSE pipe is down'

malformed state.json -> failed lane, NO partial render
disconnect -> keep last-good data, mark it visibly

options.description: The red-first fixture corpus, the two load-bearing tests (unimplemented lane renders no-adapter; fake-clock staleness threshold), or the four lifecycle events I proposed enumerating for the state ledger: server restart, adapter failure window, client reconnect, state-file replaced-or-truncated.

content.text:
Scope revision taken: **the writer is in, the board adapter stays out, nothing gets cut.** Here's the delta — I'm not re-presenting the parts you didn't object to.

---

## Revision: `StateWriter` joins as unit 7

**What it is.** A typed module plus a thin CLI wrapper (`starcar car set c2 rolling --sha abc1234`, `starcar gate set plan-review reject`). The module holds all logic and gets unit-tested; the CLI shell is smoke-tested and documented. That split isn't invented — it's the precedent `board.ps1:29-39` already set in this repo for gh wrappers, so the two pieces of tooling will follow one policy instead of two.

**Three properties, each load-bearing:**

**1. Writer and reader import the same schema module.** If the writer had its own notion of the shape, we would have rebuilt the hand-maintained mirror the TypeScript decision was chosen to eliminate — writing the exact scar back into the design while congratulating ourselves for avoiding it. One schema, two importers, compiler-enforced.

**2. Writes are atomic: temp file, then rename.** Without this the poller eventually reads a half-flushed file, fails validation, and paints *"malformed state"* on a board whose state was perfectly fine — a **confident falsehood on a status surface**, which Law 1 names as the worst defect this project can ship. It would also be intermittent and blame the wrong component. Atomic rename makes the reader see only whole files, always.

**3. Validation on write does NOT retire validation on read.** `state.json` is git-tracked and hand-editable; a human, a merge conflict, or a bad rebase can still produce garbage the writer never saw. Both sides validate. Belt and braces, because the two failure paths are genuinely different.

**Consequence that changes an earlier decision: the server polls by mtime, it does not `fs.watch`.** Atomic rename swaps the inode, and `fs.watch` handles that poorly (missed events, or events on a stale handle — worse on Windows). Polling is the dumber mechanism and the correct one. The design said "poll loop" before as a default; now it's a decision with a reason attached, which is the difference between a choice and an accident.

**Ledger impact:** the writer is a one-shot process and holds no long-lived state, so it adds no ledger rows. But it *creates* the `state-file replaced-or-truncated` lifecycle event that was previously hypothetical — the ledger's fourth column now has a real mechanism behind it, and `lastGoodSnapshot` has to be verdicted against an inode swap mid-poll.

**Still explicitly cut:** git adapter, GitHub board adapter, fuel adapter, auth, persistence, framework, theming, and any history log (`state.json` is a current-state snapshot; a REJECT round is a counter, not an event stream).

**Revised cars:** A — schema + adapter + assembler · B — writer + CLI · C — server + SSE + poll + ledger · D — view + staleness + honest-empty panels.

---

The data contract is already specified to type-level precision, so a separate spec would largely restate it, and CLAUDE.md warns that maximum ceremony on every change is itself a process failure.

content.text:
Of course — what would you like to clarify?

To make that easier, here's what's actually on the table in that question, in case the ambiguity is mine:

- **The design itself** — whether the seven units, the `LaneState` contract, the writer's shape, and the atomic-write/poll-not-watch calls are right.
- **The ladder shape** — whether design and spec collapse into one document or stay as two gates. This is purely a question of how many adversarial gates the founding contracts pass through before code gets written.
- **The dispatch count** — ~12 vs ~14 agent dispatches, which is the spend you'd be approving. I quoted it as a range because a REJECT at any gate adds a round, and REJECTs are expected outcomes here, not failures.

Anything else you want to poke at first — the cost basis, what a "car" actually costs in practice, whether four cars is the right split of that work, whether the state file schema should be nailed down before any of this, or something I haven't raised at all — ask and I'll answer straight rather than steering you back to the menu.

input.old_string: ## Dispatch rules (multi-agent hygiene)
input.new_string:
## Branch topology and landing (STANDING ORDER)

**Never commit to `main`.** Nothing reaches `main` except by pull request. `main` is the
published face of a repo whose whole thesis is that the process is visible and real, so
its history is a claim about how the work was done, and a direct commit is that claim
being made falsely.

**Documentation lands the same way code does, through the same gates.** In this project
the documents ARE the product as much as the code is: designs, specs, plans, review
verdicts, ledgers, and the operating rules themselves. A doc that skips review because
"it is only prose" is the highest-leverage unreviewed artifact in the pipeline - plans,
cars, and reviewers all consume it as ground truth. Prose gets no discount.

- Work integrates on `dev`. Car branches cut from `dev`, merge back to `dev`.
- `dev` reaches `main` by PR, reviewed, never by fast-forward push.
- The rule is enforced by branch protection on the remote, not by memory. A prose rule
  binds whoever reads it; a protected branch binds every actor, including a future agent
  that never read this file.

*Scar: on founding day the repo's first five commits went straight to `main` because no
one had said not to yet, and the session was one approval away from committing the
founding design document the same way - the document every later contract would have
inherited as ground truth. Caught by the owner, not by a gate. The order was issued
before the design landed, which is the only reason it cost nothing.*

## Dispatch rules (multi-agent hygiene)

**The order:** never commit to \`main\`. Code reaches \`main\` by pull request only. And documentation is as critical as the code - it lands through the same gates, with no discount for being prose.

**Why it matters here specifically:** this repo's thesis is that the process is visible and real, so \`main\`'s history is itself a claim about how the work was done. A direct commit makes that claim falsely. And in a project whose deliverables are largely documents (designs, specs, plans, verdicts, ledgers), exempting prose from review would exempt most of the product.

**Done:**
- \`dev\` branch created and pushed; work integrates there, car branches cut from and merge back to \`dev\`.
- Rule written into \`CLAUDE.md\` as a STANDING ORDER section with its scar attached (Healing Loop step 4: the written system is the only system that survives the night).

**Open:**

- Nothing yet enforces that a PR touching \`docs/\` got a review. That is a CI-time check and stays parked until CI lands."

Documentation gets no discount for being prose. In this project the
documents are the product as much as the code - and a spec that skips
review is the most expensive unreviewed artifact in the pipeline, because
plans, cars, and reviewers all consume it as ground truth.

Branch protection (the mechanical guard, Healing Loop step 2) is the open
half of this and is proposed to the owner separately - a prose rule binds
whoever reads it, a protected branch binds every actor.

questions.question: How should branch protection on main be configured? GitHub does not permit approving your own pull request, so on a solo-owner repo "require 1 approval" does not mean careful - it means every PR is unmergeable until a second human account approves it.

options.label: Require PR, 0 approvals, admins exempt (Recommended)
options.description: Forces every change through a PR and blocks force-push and deletion on main, but requires no approval click and leaves you able to override in an emergency. Binds agents absolutely - no agent can push to main by any path - while honoring Law 2, the dispatcher outranks the board and the system never resists an override. The real review gate is the adversarial reviewer, not GitHub's approve button.

content.text:
**The fault injection caught a real failure. The push succeeded.**

```
remote: Bypassed rule violations for refs/heads/main:
remote: - Changes must be made through a pull request.
   1729eb7..b6443c1  tmp/protection-probe -> main
=== EXIT CODE: 0 ===
```

`main` now carries `b6443c1` and a `.protection-probe` file. GitHub didn't refuse — it logged the violation and **let it through**.

**Root cause:** I authenticate as `polecatspeaks`, whose permission on this repo is `admin: true`. We set `enforce_admins: false`. So the rule was evaluated, matched, and bypassed on identity.

**The class, not the instance:** *an exemption keyed to identity protects nothing when every actor shares that identity.* Agents in this repo act **with your credential**. There is no technical distinction between "the owner pushing" and "an agent pushing" — they are the same token. So "admins exempt" and "nobody is bound" are the same sentence, and the guard was decorative from the moment it was applied.

**That recommendation was mine, and the preview I wrote said `agents: cannot reach main, period`. That was false.** I derived it from Law 2 — the dispatcher outranks the board, the system never resists an override — but I misapplied the law by conflating your *authority* with your *credential*. Blocking the agent from `main` was never resisting you; your override path is the settings page, and it stays yours either way. The law argued for keeping you able to override, not for keeping the token able to.

The good news is the honest one: this cost ~90 seconds and one junk commit, on founding day, before anything depended on it. Had we trusted the API read-back — which showed `require_pr: true` and looked perfect — the first time we'd have learned it was that a car pushed to `main` mid-train.

questions.question: How do we make the guard actually bind? The constraint we now know: agents authenticate as you, with admin, so any identity-based exemption exempts everyone.

options.label: enforce_admins: true (Recommended)
options.description: The rule binds every actor including your own pushes. Your override becomes a deliberate, visible act - toggle protection off in settings, do the thing, toggle it back - rather than something a reflexive git push can do silently. This is the only setting that enforces the order given one shared credential, and it honors Law 2 properly: your authority is intact, your token is not privileged.

owner override     : settings toggle, deliberate

one credential -> one rule, no exemption to abuse

options.label: enforce_admins: true AND plan a separate agent identity
options.description: Same immediate fix, plus we open a ticket for the real structural repair: give agents their own credential (machine account or fine-grained token with push but not admin) so the exemption becomes meaningful again and you regain a genuine personal override. Costs setup and token management, and it is the honest long-term answer to a problem we only just discovered we have.

cost: token setup + rotation burden

A lying instrument poisons everything downstream of it.

input.command:
cd /c/Users/Chris/git/starcar
gh pr create --repo polecatspeaks/StarCar --base main --head fix/revert-protection-probe \
  --title "Revert the branch-protection probe commit from main" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Reverts `b6443c1`, which reached `main` by direct push on founding day.

## What happened

A STANDING ORDER was issued (never commit to `main`; everything lands by PR, documentation included). Branch protection was applied as the mechanical guard, and the live config read back exactly as intended: `require_pr: true`, force-push and deletion blocked, `enforce_admins: false`.

Per `CLAUDE.md`'s rule that a guard which passes on arrival proves nothing until you fault-inject it once and observe the failure, a deliberate violation was attempted: a throwaway commit pushed straight to `main`.

**It succeeded.**

```
remote: Bypassed rule violations for refs/heads/main:
remote: - Changes must be made through a pull request.
   1729eb7..b6443c1  tmp/protection-probe -> main
exit code: 0
```

## Root cause, classified

The agent authenticates as `polecatspeaks`, whose repo permission is `admin: true`. With `enforce_admins: false`, the rule was evaluated, matched, and bypassed on identity.

**The class:** *an exemption keyed to identity protects nothing when every actor shares that identity.* Agents here act with the owner's credential - there is no technical distinction between "the owner pushing" and "an agent pushing", because it is one token. `admins exempt` and `nobody is bound` were the same sentence.

The reasoning error underneath it: Law 2 (the dispatcher commands, the system never resists an override) was read as an argument for exempting the owner's *credential*, when it is an argument for preserving the owner's *authority*. The override path is the settings page, and it remains the owner's under either configuration. Authority and credential are not the same thing, and conflating them produced a guard that bound nobody.

## Fix

`enforce_admins: true`. The same fault was then re-injected against the corrected config:

```
remote: error: GH006: Protected branch update failed for refs/heads/main.
remote: - Changes must be made through a pull request.
 ! [remote rejected] tmp/protection-probe -> main (protected branch hook declined)
exit code: 1
```

Before-and-after on identical input. The guard is now non-vacuous, observed rather than asserted.

## Why the probe commit is reverted rather than erased

Force-pushing `main` back would have hidden the incident, required the very admin bypass being condemned, and rewritten public history in a repo whose thesis is that its history is an honest record. The probe commit and this revert both stay. The scar is the artifact.

This PR is also the first exercise of the corrected rule - it is here because a direct push is no longer possible.

Refs #4
EOF
)"

input.old_string: ## Test-Driven Development (non-negotiable)
input.new_string:
## NORTH STAR: documentation ranks equal to code

Not "documented code." Documentation and code are two halves of one deliverable, held to
one standard. Designs, specs, plans, review verdicts, ledgers, briefs, agent definitions,
and these rules themselves ARE the product - in a shop where the workforce evaporates
nightly, the written system is the only system that survives, so a defect in the writing
is a defect in the product, not a cosmetic issue. Every rule below that binds code binds
documents: review, gates, PRs, honest stops, REJECT authority.

**Documents are living, never static.** A document is true only at the moment of its
commit. The instant the code diverges from it, that document has become a lying canary -
a reader trusting a described mechanism the code no longer implements - and this project
holds that a confident falsehood on an information surface is worse than a blank one.
Therefore: **the commit that invalidates a document updates that document, in the same
commit.** Not a follow-up ticket, not a cleanup pass, not "we will true it up before the
release." The state-ledger rule below is this principle applied to one file; it
generalizes to every file.

**Meat and bones first; pretty later.** Correctness, completeness, and citation truth are
the expensive parts and come first. Formatting, prose polish, and structure-tidying are
cheap and can happen any time afterward. Never let a polish pass be mistaken for
documentation work, and never let unpolished prose delay a document that is correct.

*Scar: this order was issued on founding day after the session had committed five times
to main and stood one approval away from committing the founding design document - the
document every later contract would have inherited as ground truth - with no gate over
any of it, on the theory that prose is cheaper than code. In a project whose deliverables
are mostly documents, exempting prose from review exempts most of the product.*

## Test-Driven Development (non-negotiable)

disclosed-but-wrong does not clear); run the suites yourself rather than trusting the
report; trace any value crossing a process/serialization boundary hop-for-hop with
file:line (the sentence check); and end with a constitution check - name each law the
diff implicates with one line of evidence it is honored, or a finding where it is not.
You edit NOTHING, commit NOTHING, push NOTHING.

Reviewers hold documents to the code standard, because here they rank equal to it:
- A diff that invalidates a document and leaves it stale is a MAJOR finding. Name the
  document, the line, and the claim the code no longer honors.
- Open every file:line a document cites and confirm it says what the citation claims.
  Uncheckable claims and dead citations are findings; a document's accuracy is testable,
  so test it rather than reading for tone.
- When the diff IS a document (design, spec, plan, ledger), review it as an artifact with
  consequences: ambiguity is a finding because a requirement readable two ways will be
  read both ways by different cars. Judge the meat, not the polish - unpolished prose
  that is correct passes; elegant prose that is wrong does not.
- A guard, gate, or protection the diff claims to install is unproven until someone has
  WATCHED it fire. Demand the fault-injection evidence, or raise the missing proof as a
  finding. A configuration read-back is an assertion, not an observation.
