## RULINGS ON THE FIVE OPEN QUESTIONS

**Q1, poll interval: config value or constant? Ruling: a constructor parameter with an exported default constant, plus a single `STARCAR_POLL_MS` environment override read once at startup. Not a config file.**
Reasons: (a) the staleness test (design:192) already requires injectable timing, so the parameter must exist for testability regardless of the Law 7 argument, which makes the constant-only option strictly more expensive, not simpler; (b) Law 7 wants a stranger to tune it and the marginal cost is one parameter; (c) §8 (design:217-221) excludes config infrastructure, so an env var is the right weight. **Additional binding requirement: the effective interval must appear in `YardSnapshot` and be rendered**, because Law 5 (`constitution.md:39-41`) makes the board's own operating parameters part of its self-knowledge, and because it is the number a stranger needs when the board looks slow.

**Q2, state file location and configurability? Ruling: default to a repo-root-relative path resolved from the process CWD, overridable by `--state <path>` and `STARCAR_STATE`, with the resolved ABSOLUTE path carried in `YardSnapshot` and rendered on the board. Separately: the live state file and the demo fixture must be two different files.**
Reasons: (a) Law 7 forbids hardcoded paths; (b) Law 5 requires the board to say what it is reading, and the single likeliest stranger failure mode is a board saying `failed: ENOENT` while the human cannot tell which path it looked at, which is a Law 5 self-knowledge failure with a one-field fix; (c) **the fixture separation is the substantive half of this ruling.** D3 (design:31) collapses "the live conductor state file" and "the in-repo synthetic demo fixture" into one artifact, and design:139-141 leans on it being git-tracked. As one file, every `starcar car set` dirties the working tree mid-train and every car's diff contains board noise, and the demo data is whatever state the last train left behind rather than a curated illustration. Split them: a curated `fixtures/demo-state.json` used by tests and by a documented demo mode, and a live state file whose tracked-or-ignored status the design states explicitly. Given the showcase thesis I would track the live file too, but that must be a stated decision, and the design must say whether the writer commits (it should not; a human does).

**Q3, second staleness threshold? Ruling: NO. One threshold in v0, and render `ageMs` numerically so the human performs the second-tier judgment themselves.**
Reasons: (a) the Healing Loop's anti-autoimmune edge (`the-healing-loop.md:73-78`): "imported ceremony that never caught anything here is a pruning candidate," and a second tier has caught nothing yet because nothing has run; (b) Law 3 (`constitution.md:30`): "Decoration that does not inform is cut," and two shades of yellow inform less than one number; (c) it costs nothing to defer, because `ageMs` is already on the wire (design:75), so a second tier is a pure view change later with zero schema churn. This is the one place in the whole design where I would spend *less* ceremony, and the author was right to ask.

**Q4, is `connectedClients` ledger-worthy? Ruling: YES. Ledger it.**
Reasons: (a) `docs/templates/state-ledger.md:11-14` names the exact failure mode of the opposite ruling: an audit of the ancestor project "found 99 latent instances of the same pattern, each individually 'too small to ledger'." The argument "it is only an implementation detail of the SSE layer" is verbatim the argument that produced ninety-nine bugs; (b) it is genuinely mutable, genuinely long-lived, and it changes across three of the four enumerated lifecycle events; (c) there is a real bug it catches: clients not removed on disconnect leak, and the leak is invisible until the "client reconnect" row forces someone to verdict it; (d) the cost of a ledger row is one line, and the cost of establishing the exemption precedent on the very first ledger this project writes is unbounded. **A ledger's first entry sets its threshold forever; set it low.**

**Q5, must "server says stale" and "I am disconnected" be visually distinct? Ruling: YES, distinct, and they must be capable of being displayed simultaneously.**
Reasons, and the decisive one is structural, not aesthetic: **the two conditions live at different scopes.** Staleness is a per-lane property produced by the assembler and carried inside `LaneState` (design:74-75); disconnection is a whole-board property of the client's transport and belongs to no lane. Merging them into "one degraded treatment" is a category error before it is a design choice: it forces the view either to paint a board-level condition onto four lanes or to hide a lane-level condition behind a board banner. Beyond that: (a) Law 6 (`constitution.md:49-50`) requires the board to SHOW disagreement rather than pick a winner, and D7 (design:35) already establishes these as two independent truths, so collapsing them in pixels discards a truth the data deliberately preserves; (b) both can be true at once (a lane went stale, *then* the connection dropped), and a single treatment cannot express that; (c) the actionability difference is total: "stale" means go look at the conductor or the adapter, "disconnected" means go look at the server or your network. Law 3 (`constitution.md:27-28`) is about shortening the path from state to decision, and these are two different decisions. **The design must also state where the disconnect indicator lives** (board chrome, not lane panels), which it currently does not.

## NORTH STAR CHECK

**Documents this design leaves stale, or will:**

1. **`README.md:8-11`** goes false the moment the server runs. `CLAUDE.md:78-79` requires the same commit. No car owns it. (MAJOR-7b.)
2. **`docs/templates/gating-matrix.md`** never instantiated despite this train landing four gated surfaces including the template's own example row. (MAJOR-7a.)
3. **`docs/setup.md:35`** GitNexus row: trigger is "FIRST CODE lands." Fires on this train. Unmentioned.
4. **`docs/setup.md:36`** CI row: trigger is "first workflow need (likely: first code PR)." Fires on this train, and it carries the quickstart runner (#6) that `CLAUDE.md:56-60` designates as the enforcement mechanism for the North Star itself. Unmentioned. Without CI, `CLAUDE.md:228`'s definition of "verified" cannot be met by any car in this train, which should be recorded as a known degradation rather than discovered at merge.
5. **Pre-existing, verified:** `README.md:51` (constitution "DRAFT", actually RATIFIED per `constitution.md:3`) and `docs/setup.md:25` ("admins exempt", live API returns `enforce_admins: true`). Minor-13 and Minor-14.

**Is commenting issue #1 sufficient?** No, on two counts. First, a comment does not un-say a body: issue #1's body still reads "ROUTE: v0 static mock first to validate the layout", and a reader who scans the body and stops (which is most readers) gets the superseded route as ground truth. The body should be edited with a dated amendment note pointing at the comment and the design, so the primary surface is true. Second, and more importantly, the same living-document logic applies to items 1-4 above, which the design does not mention at all. The design correctly identified the one stale document that was in front of it and missed the four that its own landing will create.

## WHAT IS GOOD

Stated plainly, and limited to what genuinely is. Three of the attacks I was told to run failed, and the reasons matter for the re-review:

- **Every law citation is correct.** I opened all eleven. Not one wrong number, and the paraphrases are faithful to the text rather than convenient. The only citation defect in the document is a line range in a PowerShell file whose underlying claim is true.
- **design:79-93 is the real thing.** Four required keys plus a discriminated union genuinely does convert Law 4 from a review rule into a compile error, and the design's own justification (`the-healing-loop.md:36-40`, structural impossibility over remembered guards) is correctly applied. I attacked this and it held. My only finding against it is that the union is missing one member, which is a completeness defect in a fundamentally sound structure, not a shape defect.
- **Health inside the return type (design:44-56)** rather than as a side channel is the same move applied a second time, independently, and it is right: a lane cannot be rendered without carrying its own health because the type will not let you separate them.
- **D6 (design:34) is correct and correctly reasoned.** "Polling is the dumber mechanism and the correct one," argued from the inode swap that atomic rename produces. That is exactly the right instinct. My only complaint is that the design does not apply that same instinct one hop later at the browser boundary: it never records why SSE beat a browser-side poll of `/api/snapshot`, an alternative that would delete MAJOR-4 in its entirety. I am not rejecting on the SSE choice, which is defensible, but the design owes a rejected-alternative line, and if SSE stays then all three sub-findings of MAJOR-4 must be closed.
- **§10 is honest and it worked.** Question 4 in particular ("argued both ways; the reviewer should rule") is a real question with a real cost either way. A design that hides its soft spots produces a review that finds nothing.
- **Right-sizing: the four-car split and the full ladder are correct for this scope, and I looked hard for the autoimmune direction.** `CLAUDE.md:18-22` reserves the full ladder for "new subsystems, rewrites, cross-boundary contracts," and this is all three at once, in the document every later contract inherits. Fourteen dispatches for a train that defines the wire format, the adapter seam, the state ledger, and the test policy is proportionate. The cars are split at genuine seams (schema/adapter/assembler, writer, server, view) and none is trivial. **The problem with this train is not too much process. It is that the specification load is distributed unevenly across the cars** (Minor-5: Car A gets three sections and an interface block, Car D gets one paragraph and the largest rendering matrix in the system), and that roughly ten contracts the cars need are still undetermined. The spec rung is genuinely load-bearing here, which is itself evidence the ladder is right-sized rather than ceremonial.

---

## RE-REVIEW GATE

For APPROVE on the next round, all nine Majors must be closed in the design document, not deferred to the spec, because each one determines a contract that cars A through D inherit as ground truth. Minor-1 (citation), Minor-3 (the false categorical), Minor-4 (Law 2), and Minor-6 (Law 4 vs Law 7 trade) are one-sentence fixes and should land in the same revision. Minor-13 and Minor-14 are pre-existing repo defects outside this design's scope; I raise them because the North Star check asked and I verified both against the live remote, and they belong in a separate sweep, not in this design's revision.

content.text:
Swept and pushed. Now the verdict.

## REJECT — 9 Major, 14 Minor, and it went after exactly what I asked it to

**The load-bearing kill (MAJOR-2): my central structural claim was wrong, three independent ways.** I argued that sharing one TypeScript type across the server/browser boundary retires the sentence check there. The reviewer took it apart:

1. **"A duplicate definition is a build failure" is simply false.** TypeScript is *structurally* typed. A second, divergent `YardSnapshot` in the view compiles clean, interops clean, and emits no diagnostic — especially here, where the value arrives from `JSON.parse` and gets cast. So my §6 "shared-type test" would have **passed the moment it was written**, against a codebase with two conflicting definitions. `CLAUDE.md`: *a test that passes the moment you write it proves nothing.* I wrote a vacuous test into the design as its structural guarantee.
2. **`JSON.parse` is an unchecked cast.** I put validators in the schema module and applied them on read and on write — and never at the wire. `ageMs: number` becomes `null` if it's ever `Infinity`/`NaN`. `lastGood?: T` — `undefined` keys vanish in `stringify`, so "explicitly cleared" and "absent" are indistinguishable.
3. **The SSE event name is a hand-written string on both sides.** One character apart and the listener never fires, the socket stays **open**, so the client reports itself *connected* while displaying the first paint forever. A permanent silent lie, generated entirely inside a fully type-checked pipeline.

That is the sentence-check scar exactly — nine wire fields through sixteen correct files. **I didn't delete the class. I renamed it and declared the renamed thing safe.**

**The most elegant catch (MAJOR-1):** my proudest move — four required keys in a discriminated union so a missing lane is a *compile error* — built a **closed** union with no member for "not yet known." Server's up, first poll hasn't returned, browser hits `/api/snapshot`: `ok` and `stale` need data we don't have, `failed` needs a failure that didn't happen, and `no-adapter` is a **lie**. Meanwhile `constitution.md:17` says, in the first law, in plain words: *"Unknown states render AS unknown, honestly."* The structure I built to enforce Law 4 cannot express the state Law 1 names by hand.

**The one that indicts the whole train (MAJOR-7):** a four-car, fourteen-dispatch train with **zero documentation scope**, in the repo whose north star is that documentation ranks equal to code. `README.md` goes false the instant the server runs, and no car owns it. It also caught that I instantiated one of our two contract templates and dropped the other — the **gating matrix**, whose own worked example row is *the staleness banner this very train builds*.

**And then it found mine.** `setup.md:25` — I wrote "admins stay exempt so the owner can always override," changed the control ninety minutes later, and never swept the doc. It queried the live API to prove it. A lying canary about a **security control**, in the file a stranger reads to learn what guards this repo, written by the person who'd authored the living-document rule two hours earlier. Swept in `e236252`. The rule wasn't weak; I was.

**On the open questions it ruled cleanly, and one line is going into the institution:** on whether `connectedClients` is too small to ledger — *"A ledger's first entry sets its threshold forever; set it low."* It also killed my second staleness threshold as autoimmune ceremony (correct), and ruled that the live state file and the demo fixture must be **two different artifacts** — because as one file, every `starcar car set` dirties the tree mid-train and the "demo data" is just whatever the last train left lying around.

**The diff I promised you.** It caught contracts and implementability — deep. I'd flagged the "can the schema render *our own reality*" gaps — wide. Almost no overlap; the one intersection is Law 2, which I framed as "human interventions aren't first-class yard events" and it framed as "the second-ranked law is absent from both the design *and* the out-of-scope list." Two different lenses, one shared blind spot found from opposite directions. That's the doctrine-convergence signal the Healing Loop says means the system is working.

I can take eight of the nine Majors myself. One is genuinely yours:

questions.question: MAJOR-3 is a real contradiction in my design and the fix is a scope call, not a technical one. The StateWriter CLI I specified includes `starcar gate set plan-review reject`, the schema defines `Gate`, and the state file therefore holds gate data - while the design simultaneously declares the signals lane has "no adapter" and renders it as an empty honest-empty panel. Rendering `no-adapter` over gate data the system demonstrably holds is itself the Law 4 violation the design cites Law 4 to justify. Which way does it reconcile?

Costs almost nothing extra: the writer, schema, and adapter already handle gates, so only the view work grows. It also makes the board immediately useful for the thing this repo produces most - gate verdicts - and gets a REJECT rendered on screen, which is Law 3's own worked example of what the board is for.

content.text:
What would you like to clarify?

To lay out what's actually behind the question, in case the ambiguity is mine:

**The contradiction, concretely.** I specified a writer CLI with `starcar gate set plan-review reject`, and a schema containing `Gate`. That means gate verdicts get written into `state.json`. The `StateFileAdapter` reads that whole file. So the system *holds* gate data — while my design simultaneously says the signals lane has no adapter and paints it as an empty panel. Those can't both be true, and the reviewer's point is that painting `no-adapter` over data we're holding is a Law 4 violation committed by the very section that invokes Law 4.

**Why it's your call and not mine.** All three fixes are technically fine. What differs is what v0 *is*. Option 1 makes the board show REJECTs on day one — which is arguably the single most valuable pixel in this whole project, and Law 3's own worked example of what a board is for. Option 2 keeps the train at the size you approved. Option 3 splits the difference at the cost of a fifth union member and two empty-states a stranger can't tell apart.

**The cost asymmetry, honestly stated:** the reviewer separately flagged that Car D is the *thinnest-specified car with the largest rendering surface* — four lane kinds × server-stale × client-disconnected, plus the new `unknown` state MAJOR-1 forces. Option 1 grows exactly that car. That's not a reason to refuse it, but it means Option 1 probably comes with re-splitting Car D, which nudges the train past four cars.

Things you might reasonably want to poke at instead:

- **Whether to trust this reviewer's severity calibration at all** — it returned nine Majors on a first design. That's either a good gate or an instrument crying wolf, and we have exactly one data point. I have a view.
- **Whether nine Majors means the design should be rewritten rather than revised** — our own rewrite-vs-extend rule asks whether the *shape* is the bug.
- **Whether to fix all nine before re-review, or fix and re-review in batches.**
- **Whether the spec rung absorbs some of these** — the reviewer explicitly says no, all nine must close in the design because cars inherit them as ground truth. That's arguable.
- Or something else entirely.

Ask, and I'll answer straight rather than routing you back to a menu.
ok lets look at this from less of a code does thing pov and more from a "whats the right truth to tell". Can both be positive? Sure? I guess? I mean, why not?

That reframe is better than my question, and I think it dissolves the problem rather than answering it.

**Here's what I did wrong.** I collapsed three orthogonal truths onto one axis. `ok | stale | failed | no-adapter` treats these as alternatives:

- **Is there an adapter?** (connection)
- **Did it produce data, and how fresh?** (freshness)
- **Does the board render it?** (capability)

Those aren't alternatives. They're independent facts that are *all* true at once, always. Forcing them onto one enum means every combination that isn't on my list becomes inexpressible — and then I have to pick which lie to tell.

**Watch both Major findings evaporate under the reframe:**

- **MAJOR-1** (no state for "server up, first poll hasn't returned") wasn't a missing union member. It's `connection: present · freshness: never-polled`. Ordinary. Expressible the moment freshness is its own axis.
- **MAJOR-3** (gates written but signals declared adapter-less) isn't a contradiction to resolve by picking a side. The truth is `connection: present · freshness: ok, 3 gates as of 19:41 · capability: not yet rendered`. **Three positive statements, zero lies**, and strictly more informative than any of my three options.

So: **yes, both positive.** Not as a compromise — as the correct model. My union was the compromise, and I'd built it while congratulating myself for making Law 4 a compile error.

And this is the honest diagnosis the reviewer's nine findings were circling without naming: our own rewrite-vs-extend rule says a *coverage* defect gets a rider while a **structural** defect — where the shape is the bug — goes back through the ladder. Nine Majors against one contract is the tell. I was going to patch nine holes in a container that was the wrong shape.

**The metaphor already has the word for it, which I take as a good sign.** Railroads distinguish a *dark* signal (no equipment) from a *bagged* signal — equipment that physically exists, deliberately out of service, hooded so crews know not to obey it. That's precisely `connection: present · capability: not rendered`. The vocabulary we're borrowing already solved this, because real yards have always had to say "this thing exists and is not in service" without implying it's broken *or* working.

**Two honest counterarguments, since you asked why not:**

1. **Law 3 says decoration that doesn't inform gets cut.** A lane displaying three status facts can read as noise where one word read as signal. The answer is probably that the axes are *modeled* separately but *rendered* as one resolved glance-state, with detail on demand — the dispatcher sees "bagged," not a status matrix. But that's a claim about rendering I haven't earned yet, and it's Car D's surface, which the reviewer already flagged as our thinnest-specified and largest.

2. **Where each axis lives matters for Law 6.** Connection and freshness are adapter facts, so they belong on the wire. *Capability* is the view's own property — the server has no business asserting what a browser can render. Putting it on the wire would be the server guessing about the client, which is a second copy of something that can drift. So: two axes on the wire, one local fact in the view, composed at render time. That keeps "adapters own the facts" intact.

Before I re-cut the core contract and take the other seven Majors:

questions.question: Does the re-shape land the way you meant - separate the axes so a lane can state several true things at once, rather than picking one label from a menu?

Costs one extra dispatch on a REJECT round, which the process expects.

content.text:
Where does it diverge?

Three readings I can see, and I'd rather you tell me than have me build on the wrong one:

1. **The one I built on** — a lane holds several independent truths at once, so stop picking one label. (What I just proposed.)

2. **Scope, not types** — "can both be positive" meant the *options* weren't exclusive: ship gates live *and* keep the train honest about size, rather than treating it as pick-one. I turned a scope answer into a type-system answer.

3. **The board's voice** — this one I think I may have walked past. Every empty state I designed is phrased as a **negation**: `!! NO ADAPTER CONNECTED`, loud and red. But the truth about the freight lane isn't a failure — it's *"not built yet,"* which is a plan, not an alarm. Painting an unbuilt lane in failure-red is its own dishonesty, and worse, it trains the dispatcher to ignore red. Law 3 says an element earns its pixels by shortening the path from state to decision, and a red panel that requires no action is anti-actionable. A positive voice would say what *is* — `trains: live · signals: data held, view pending · freight: planned` — and save alarm styling for things that are genuinely wrong.

If it's (3), that's a bigger correction than the axes, because it changes the board's whole register — and it would mean my "honest empty state" instinct was right about the *content* and wrong about the *tone*, which is exactly the sort of thing that ships and then annoys someone daily.

Or it's something else entirely. What's off?

so again, let's take a slightly different view, or adjust our thinking a bit. between each state is a transition phase, so can all three of the dark|bagged|TBD(under construction, inflight, next spec) as a known good accurate constant so that we can hold data with out surfacing it, but have the construct ready to plug in once we're at that point. That tracking?

Tracking. And it's a better model than mine — mine described *current truth*, yours describes **position on a declared build-out**, which is a bigger truth and one my design couldn't express at all.

What I now understand you're saying:

**These are known-good constants, not degradations.** `dark`, `bagged`, `under construction` aren't absences of a working state — they're each *correct*, declared, accurate. Which kills the alarm problem outright: nothing renders red, because nothing is wrong. A bagged signal isn't broken, it's hooded on purpose.

**The socket exists before the data does.** "Have the construct ready to plug in" means the adapter seam is *instantiated* for lanes that have nothing yet — a declared, registered, empty socket rather than a missing key. So going live is plugging in, not rebuilding. That's much stronger than my four-required-keys trick, because the socket carries its own declared position instead of the type merely forbidding omission.

**Transitions are real, and they're the part I had no representation for whatsoever.** My union had only resting states. `in flight` is not a resting state — it's *becoming*, and something is actively causing it.

And that last one has a consequence I want to put in front of you, because I think it's the best thing to come out of this conversation:

**A lane under construction is a lane with a train on it — and that train is already on the board.** "Signals lane, under construction" isn't a status string. It's a *link*: `signals ← train T2, car B, at inspection, one REJECT round`. The board renders its own build-out using the exact machinery it renders everything else with. The dogfooding loop doesn't just close, it closes *structurally* — StarCar's roadmap is a StarCar board, and the freight lane's inbound queue is literally the issues that will build the freight lane.

That also means the board stops being only a status surface and becomes a **roadmap**, which is strictly more actionable (Law 3) — "not built yet, spec'd next, nobody on it" tells a dispatcher something a red `NO ADAPTER` never did.

One thing I need before I re-cut the contract, because it changes the shape:

questions.question: Is the third bucket one state or several positions? You wrote it as TBD with three flavors - under construction, in flight, next spec - and they differ in a way that matters: "next spec" has nobody on it, while "under construction / in flight" means a train is actively on that lane right now. Whether those are one label or distinct positions decides whether the board can show WHO is building a lane and where they have got to.

options.label: Distinct positions, and in-flight links to its train (Recommended)
options.description: dark, planned, spec'd, under-construction, bagged, live. The under-construction position is not a string - it carries a reference to the train building it, so the board resolves it into the real consist and gate state it is already rendering elsewhere. The board renders its own build-out with its own machinery, and the roadmap becomes live rather than hand-maintained.

Far cheaper - one constant, one string, no link resolution, no coupling between the lane registry and yard state. Cost: the note is hand-maintained, so it rots exactly like every other hand-maintained mirror this repo has a scar about, and the board cannot resolve it into anything.

content.text: What would you like to clarify?
oh this is a schoedinger's cat problem in real life! We can't know the states we need until we observe the states we need and that changes the states we need!

Yes. And it means **the error was never the enum's contents — it was that I kept trying to enumerate.**

Look at the round we just had. I wrote four states. The reviewer found a fifth (`unknown`). You found three more (`dark`, `bagged`, `under construction`), then immediately split the third into three. Every observation produced states. That's not a series of misses converging on the right list — that's evidence the list doesn't stabilize, because **the act of running the yard is what generates the states**, and we haven't run it yet.

And here's the part that stings: **Law 7 already ruled on this.** *"Pluggable adapters, no hardcoded board schemas or label taxonomies."* I hardcoded a label taxonomy, then spent three rounds trying to get the hardcoded one right, while the ratified law sat there saying don't. The reviewer even flagged the adjacent half (four hardcoded lane keys) as a Law 4/Law 7 tension. The answer was on the books before the question was asked.

**So: close the shape, open the vocabulary.**

- **Closed** — a small, fixed set of *registers*: roughly `nominal` / `in-progress` / `needs-attention`. This is the glance-language, and it must never grow, because Law 3 lives here — the dispatcher's eye has to resolve a board in one pass, and a visual language anyone can extend decays into mush.
- **Open** — the set of *positions* (`dark`, `bagged`, `spec'd`, `under construction`, `live`, and whatever next Tuesday produces) is **data**, each declaring which register it renders in. Adding one is a data edit, not a contract change, not a migration, not a car.

**And the unknown-position path stops being an error path and becomes the instrument.** If the state file names a position the board doesn't know, it renders — loudly, honestly — *"unrecognized position: 'shopped'"*. That's Law 1 and Law 4 doing their jobs, and simultaneously it's the detector telling us we just found a new state. You don't resolve Schrödinger by guessing what's in the box. **You build the detector and open it.**

Which reframes what v0 is actually *for*. Not "render four lanes." v0's job is: **run against our own process and find out what states exist.** Ship the minimum vocabulary we've genuinely *observed* — not the maximum we can imagine — plus the discovery path, and let the yard tell us the rest. That's YAGNI enforced by epistemics rather than by taste: we don't know, so we don't guess, we instrument. And the first corpus is sitting right here — this session already produced a guard that passed vacuously, a route superseded by owner ruling, a reviewer working a stale base, and a REJECT with nine Majors. None of those fit any list I'd have written.

It also retires the question you just declined to answer, which is the tell that it's the right cut: `under construction / in flight / next spec` stop being a shape decision I need from you. They're vocabulary entries. We add them when we see them, and if we're wrong the cost is a data edit.

That's a real change to the design's core, so I want to say plainly what it costs before I go: the contract gets *smaller* and the ladder gets *cheaper* — but v0 ships knowing less, on purpose, and the board will visibly say "I don't recognize this" in front of you, which will feel like a defect the first few times it happens. It isn't. It's the thing working.

Unless you redirect me again, I'll re-cut the design on this — closed registers, open positions, unknown-as-detector — fold in the seven remaining Majors, and send it back through the same reviewer. Does that land, or is there another turn of the screw here?
send it!
Re-cutting the design now. Revision 2 addresses all nine Majors, adopts the reviewer's five rulings, and re-shapes the core contract around closed-mechanism/open-vocabulary.

input.content:
# Design: v0 yard skeleton

Status: **DRAFT rev 2 - awaiting adversarial design review (round 2)**
Issue: #1 (`area:view`)
Date: 2026-07-21
Ladder rung: design (rung 1 of: design → spec → plan → cars)
History: rev 1 REJECTed, 9 Major / 14 Minor. See §12 for what changed and why.

## 1. What this train delivers

A running StarCar: a local server process that reads a conductor-maintained state file
and renders the yard live in a browser, with every declared lane present from the first
commit - whether or not it has data yet.

**The organising idea of rev 2:** a lane is a *socket with a declared position on a
build-out*, not a payload that is either present or absent. The socket exists before the
data does. Going live later is plugging in, not rebuilding.

## 2. The epistemic constraint that shapes everything below

We cannot enumerate the states this board needs, because **running the yard is what
generates them**. Rev 1 declared four lane states. Its adversarial review found a fifth.
The owner then found three more, and immediately split the third into three again. That
is not a sequence of near-misses converging on a correct list; it is evidence the list
does not stabilise before observation.

Law 7 already ruled on this and rev 1 disobeyed it: *"pluggable adapters, **no hardcoded
board schemas or label taxonomies**"* (`constitution.md:54`). Rev 1 hardcoded a label
taxonomy and then spent three rounds trying to get the hardcoded one right.

**The rule this design adopts: close what MECHANISM determines, open what the SHOP
names.**

- A poll has either never run, or last succeeded, or last failed. That set is closed by
  mechanism and provably complete, so it is a compile-time union.
- What a lane is *called* when it holds data it does not surface - `bagged`, `hooded`,
  `staged`, whatever the next shop says - is vocabulary. That set is open by nature, so it
  is **data**.

**And the unrecognised-value path is not an error path; it is the detector.** When the
board meets a position it does not know, it renders that fact loudly and by name. That is
Law 1 and Law 4 doing their jobs, and simultaneously it is the instrument that tells us we
have just observed a state we had not enumerated. You do not resolve an
observation-dependent state space by guessing harder. You build the detector and open the
box.

**Therefore v0's actual job is discovery.** It ships the minimum vocabulary we have
genuinely observed - not the maximum we can imagine - plus the path that surfaces the
rest. The board saying *"I do not recognise this"* in front of the owner is the design
working, not failing.

## 3. Decisions taken, with their reasons

| # | Decision | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| D1 | Live server + browser | The board is a wall display glanced at, not a page refreshed. Cost accepted: lifecycle, staleness and reconnect are first-class work now. |
| D2 | TypeScript on Node, **with runtime validation at every boundary** | One language lets the server and view share a type *declaration*. It does NOT make the wire safe - see §5.2. Rev 1's claim that it did was its central error. |
| D3 | Conductor state file is the first adapter | It is the only source that *knows* a REJECT happened; git records no verdicts, and inferring them would violate Law 6. |
| D4 | Every declared lane is present in every snapshot, in its declared position | Law 4. See §5.1 for how this is guarded now that lanes are a registry rather than fixed keys. |
| D5 | A writer for the state file ships in this train | Hand-editing JSON mid-train is hand-maintained state at a process boundary. §5.6. |
| D6 | The server polls by mtime; it does not `fs.watch` | Atomic rename swaps the inode, which `fs.watch` handles poorly - missed events or events on a stale handle, worse on Windows. |
| D7 | Data-staleness (server) and connection-loss (client) stay two separate truths, simultaneously displayable | They fail independently and live at different scopes: staleness is per-lane and comes from the assembler; disconnection is whole-board and belongs to the transport. Merging them is a category error before it is a design choice. |
| D8 | **Registers are closed; positions are open** | §2. The glance-language must be stable (Law 3) while the vocabulary must be pluggable (Law 7). |
| D9 | **SSE is chosen over browser-side polling of `/api/snapshot`** | Recorded as a rejected alternative because rev 1's review noted that browser polling would delete §5.3's entire problem set. SSE wins on the wall-board case: an idle yard costs nothing and a change appears without a round-trip. The price is the heartbeat, ordering and change-detection rules in §5.3, all of which are now specified rather than assumed. |
| D10 | **The toolchain car runs FIRST** | `CLAUDE.md`: *"verified means the pipeline that ships it went green."* Until CI exists, no car in this train can honestly claim verification. §10. |

## 4. The contract

### 4.1 Registers - CLOSED, and this is the only closed taxonomy in the system

```ts
type Register = 'nominal' | 'in-progress' | 'needs-attention'
```

The glance-language. Law 3 lives here: a dispatcher resolves the board in one pass, and a
visual vocabulary that anyone may extend decays into mush. Growing this set is a
constitution-level decision, not a config edit.

### 4.2 Positions - OPEN, shipped as data

```ts
type PositionDef = {
  id: string             // 'live' | 'bagged' | 'dark' | 'under-construction' | ...
  label: string          // what the board prints
  register: Register     // how it renders at a glance
  surfacesData: boolean  // does this lane show its payload while in this position?
}
```

Ships as a data file, loaded at startup, **and carried on the wire inside the snapshot**
so the view never hardcodes a vocabulary (Law 6: the source owns the facts; Law 7: no
hardcoded taxonomies).

v0 ships only positions we have actually observed:

| id | label | register | surfacesData |
|---|---|---|---|
| `live` | Live | `nominal` | true |
| `bagged` | Data held, not surfaced | `nominal` | false |
| `dark` | No equipment | `nominal` | false |
| `under-construction` | Being built | `in-progress` | false |

`bagged` is the railroad term for a signal that physically exists and is deliberately out
of service, hooded so crews know not to obey it. It is exactly the state rev 1 could not
express, and it is not a degradation - it is correct, declared and accurate.

**The detector:** a position id present in the state file or lane registry that resolves
to no `PositionDef` is rendered as `needs-attention`, labelled verbatim
`unrecognised position: '<id>'`. It is never silently coerced to a default, never hidden,
and never rendered as an error in the software. It is a discovery.

### 4.3 Freshness - CLOSED, because mechanism determines it

```ts
type Freshness<T> =
  | { kind: 'never-polled' }
  | { kind: 'fresh';  data: T; asOf: string }
  | { kind: 'stale';  data: T; asOf: string; ageMs: number }
  | { kind: 'failed'; reason: FailureReason; lastGood?: T; lastGoodAsOf?: string }

type FailureReason = { code: string; detail: string }
```

Provably complete: a poll has never run, or its last run succeeded (and the data is
current or aged), or its last run failed. `never-polled` is what rev 1's review found
missing, and `constitution.md:17` names it by hand: *"Unknown states render AS unknown,
honestly."*

`FailureReason` is structured rather than free prose so the view can render `file-missing`
differently from `schema-violation` without parsing English.

### 4.4 Lane and snapshot

```ts
type Lane = {
  id: string
  title: string
  position: string        // a PositionDef id - MAY be unrecognised
  freshness: Freshness<unknown>
}

type YardSnapshot = {
  seq: number             // monotonic, increments per emitted snapshot
  asOf: string | null     // null before the first poll completes
  config: {               // Law 5: the board knows and states its own parameters
    pollMs: number
    heartbeatMs: number
    stalenessMs: number
    statePath: string     // resolved ABSOLUTE path
    demoMode: boolean
  }
  positions: PositionDef[]
  lanes: Lane[]
}
```

### 4.5 The Law 4 / Law 7 trade, recorded explicitly

Rev 1 made `lanes` four required keys so that omitting one was a compile error. That was a
genuinely good guard and it is being **weakened on purpose**, because four fixed keys is a
hardcoded board schema and Law 7 forbids one by name.

Law 4 outranks Law 7, so this is only legitimate if Law 4 is still honoured by other
means. It is: lanes come from a **registry**, the assembler iterates the registry rather
than constructing keys by hand, and a red-first test asserts *every registered lane
appears in every snapshot, in every code path, including before the first poll*. Nothing
can be silently dropped; the guarantee simply moved from the compiler to a test.

Trade stated so a later reviewer does not have to reconstruct it: **we exchanged a
compile-time guarantee for pluggability, and bought back a mechanical runtime guard.** A
stranger can now add a fifth lane without editing a core type.

### 4.6 `Adapter<T>` - the seam

```ts
type AdapterResult<T> =
  | { ok: true;  data: T; asOf: string }
  | { ok: false; reason: FailureReason }

interface Adapter<T> {
  readonly name: string
  poll(): Promise<AdapterResult<T>>
}
```

An adapter never throws into the view, and its health is part of its return value rather
than a side channel - a lane cannot be rendered without carrying its own health, because
the type will not let you separate them.

## 5. Units

### 5.1 `schema` + registries

One module defining every type above, both validators, the SSE event-name constant, and
the single serialisation function. Imported by adapter, writer, server and view. The
single definition is the guard, and §7 makes it mechanical.

The **lane registry** and **position vocabulary** are data files loaded at startup. The
lane registry declares which lanes exist and each one's current position; it changes on
deploy, not at runtime. Two distinct fact domains, deliberately not merged:

- **Lane registry** - the product's build-out truth. Owned by the project.
- **State file** - yard events: trains, cars, gates. Owned by the conductor.

`Yard` (the state file's root type) carries `trains` and `gates` in v0. Gates are written,
stored, read and held - and the signals lane's position is `bagged`, which is the honest
statement that we hold gate data and do not yet surface it. Rev 1's contradiction (a
writer that wrote gates beside a lane declared adapter-less) dissolves rather than being
resolved by cutting a feature.

### 5.2 Wire safety - rev 1's central error, and its repair

Rev 1 claimed that sharing a TypeScript type across the server/browser boundary reduced
the sentence check to "confirm the type is genuinely shared." **That claim was wrong in
three independent ways**, and the repairs are binding requirements on this train:

1. **A duplicate type declaration is NOT a build failure.** TypeScript is structurally
   typed; a second, divergent `YardSnapshot` in the view compiles clean and emits no
   diagnostic, especially here where the value arrives from `JSON.parse` and is cast.
   Rev 1's "shared-type test" would have passed the moment it was written.
   **Repair:** an ESLint `no-restricted-syntax` rule banning declaration of these type
   names outside `src/schema.ts`, plus a source scan asserting each resolves to exactly
   one file. Both fail red against a two-definition codebase.
2. **`JSON.parse` is an unchecked cast.** A shared compile-time type constrains what the
   source code *claims*; it constrains nothing that crosses the wire. `ageMs: number`
   becomes `null` if it is ever `Infinity` or `NaN`; `lastGood?: T` loses the distinction
   between explicitly-cleared and absent, because `JSON.stringify` drops `undefined` keys.
   **Repair:** the view runs the shared runtime validator on every parsed payload.
   A divergence is a red test, not a silent render.
3. **The SSE event name is a hand-written string on both sides.** One character apart and
   the listener never fires, the socket stays **open**, and the client therefore reports
   itself connected while displaying the first paint forever - a permanent silent lie
   generated inside a fully type-checked pipeline.
   **Repair:** the event name is an exported constant in `schema`, imported by both sides,
   with a test asserting the server writes the constant the client subscribes to.

Additionally, `/api/snapshot` and `/api/stream` are two producers of one payload, which is
a hand-maintained mirror by another name. **Both serialise through one exported function,
with a test asserting byte-identical output for a fixed snapshot.**

The sentence check for this train is therefore NOT retired. Its hops are: assembler
output → serialise → HTTP body / SSE `data:` frame → `JSON.parse` → runtime validator →
render. Per-car reviewers trace all six.

### 5.3 `Server`

- `GET /` and static assets.
- `GET /api/snapshot` - current snapshot, for first paint.
- `GET /api/stream` - SSE.
- Poll loop at `pollMs`.

Three rules rev 1 left undefined, each of which produced a silent lie:

**Change detection.** Emit when the snapshot's canonical serialisation differs from the
last emitted one, **excluding `ageMs` and `asOf`** (which change every poll by
construction) and **including `freshness.kind`** (so the `fresh` → `stale` transition
emits even though no data changed). Rev 1's "emits only on change" had two readings: one
made the stated benefit fiction, the other meant a client that connected while fresh would
display `fresh` forever while the server knew otherwise - `constitution.md:42-43` verbatim.

**Heartbeat.** An SSE comment line every `heartbeatMs`. The client flips to disconnected
after two missed heartbeats. `heartbeatMs` travels in `snapshot.config` so the client does
not hardcode it. Without this, a half-open TCP connection (laptop sleep, wifi drop) leaves
`EventSource` in `readyState === OPEN` indefinitely with no error event, and the
disconnect row in §6 is unbacked in the commonest real disconnect mode.

**Ordering.** `seq` is monotonic. The client applies a snapshot only if `seq` exceeds the
last applied. Without it, a first-paint fetch that resolves after an early stream event
overwrites newer state with older and renders it as current.

**Poll concurrency.** A poll that outlasts the interval must not stack: `pollInFlight`
guards, and an overlapping tick is **skipped, not queued**. Two concurrent reads
completing out of order would let an older read overwrite the newer snapshot.

### 5.4 `SnapshotAssembler`

```ts
interface SnapshotAssembler {
  assemble(input: {
    results: Map<string, AdapterResult<unknown>>
    previous: YardSnapshot | null
    now: () => number          // injected clock - required for the staleness test
    stalenessMs: number
  }): YardSnapshot
}
```

Pure. Owns staleness classification and nothing else derives it - the view never computes
it (Law 6). `lastGood` is read from `previous`, so per-lane last-good and the server's
last-good snapshot are **one copy, not two**; rev 1 had two granularities of the same idea,
which Law 6 forbids by name.

The injected clock exists because rev 1 named a "fake clock" staleness test against a unit
with no interface block to inject one into.

### 5.5 `View`

Plain TypeScript and DOM, no framework, tested under jsdom.

Renders every lane in the snapshot, resolving `position` against the shipped `positions`
vocabulary, then composing three facts into one glance-state plus detail on demand:

- **position** (from the registry) - what this lane *is* on the build-out.
- **freshness** (from the adapter) - what its data is doing.
- **capability** (local to the view) - whether a renderer exists for this lane's payload.

Capability is deliberately **view-local and never on the wire**: the server has no business
asserting what a browser can render, and putting it on the wire would be a second copy of
something that can drift.

Register drives colour and weight. **Nothing renders in an alarm register unless something
is genuinely wrong** - an unbuilt lane is a plan, not a failure, and painting it red trains
the dispatcher to ignore red. Law 3.

Board chrome (not lane panels) carries: `asOf`, the connection indicator, the resolved
state path, the effective `pollMs`, and the DEMO banner when `demoMode` is set.
Simultaneous display of "this lane is stale" and "I am disconnected" is required, because
both can be true and they imply different actions - go look at the conductor, versus go
look at the server.

Law 3 obligation, called out because rev 1's review found this unit the thinnest-specified
with the largest surface: **a REJECT must be visible before any report is read, and a
stalled car must look stalled at a glance.** That is the law's own worked example and this
project's whole premise.

### 5.6 `StateWriter`

A typed module plus a thin CLI (`starcar car set c2 rolling --sha abc1234`,
`starcar gate set plan-review reject`). The module holds the logic and is unit-tested; the
CLI shell is smoke-tested, which here means **an automated end-to-end run against a temp
directory** (this project's writer has no external service, so `board.ps1:9-14`'s
precedent - do not unit-test a wrapper whose only untested part is a live API - transfers
only partly, and the automatable half is automated).

Three binding properties:

1. **Writer and reader import one schema module.** A writer with its own notion of the
   shape rebuilds the hand-maintained mirror D2 exists to eliminate.
2. **Writes are atomic: temp file, then rename.** Otherwise the poller reads a half-flushed
   file, fails validation, and paints "malformed state" over state that was fine - a
   confident falsehood on a status surface, intermittent, blaming the wrong component.
3. **Validation on write does not retire validation on read.** The file is hand-editable;
   a human, a bad merge or a rebase can produce garbage the writer never saw.

The writer stamps `writtenAt` inside the file (§5.7). The writer never commits; a human
does.

### 5.7 `asOf` provenance

`asOf` derives from a **`writtenAt` field inside the state file**, stamped by the writer -
never from filesystem mtime, because `git checkout` and `git clone` rewrite mtime to now,
which would make a three-day-old fixture read as perfectly fresh.

Guards, both red-first:

- **A future-dated `writtenAt` is `failed`**, code `timestamp-in-future`, not `fresh`.
  Rev 1 anticipated hand-editing, and a hand-typed future timestamp yields a negative age
  that never crosses the threshold, pinning a lane to `fresh` permanently.
- **A clock step backwards** must not produce a negative age or a wild one. Staleness is
  computed from the injected clock and asserted non-negative; negative resolves to
  `failed`, never silently to zero.

**The live state file and the demo fixture are two different artifacts.** As one file,
every `starcar car set` dirties the working tree mid-train, every car's diff carries board
noise, and the "demo data" is whatever the last train happened to leave behind rather than
a curated illustration. `fixtures/demo-state.json` is curated, git-tracked and used by
tests and demo mode; the live state file is git-tracked too (showcase thesis) and that is
a stated decision rather than an accident. Demo mode renders a persistent DEMO banner and
treats age as informational, so a checked-in fixture does not read as a broken board.

## 6. Error handling

| Failure | Behavior | Law |
|---|---|---|
| Adapter throws | Caught at the boundary → `failed` with a coded reason. Never a blank lane. | 4 |
| Malformed state file | Coded `schema-violation` with detail. **No partial render.** | 1 |
| File missing/unreadable | Coded `file-missing` / `file-unreadable`, OS reason in detail. | 1, 4 |
| `writtenAt` in the future | Coded `timestamp-in-future`. Not `fresh`. | 1 |
| Data aged past threshold | `stale` - shown, visibly marked, `ageMs` rendered numerically. | 5 |
| SSE heartbeat missed twice | Board chrome shows disconnected, last-good stays on screen **visibly marked**. | 1, 5 |
| Wire payload fails validation | Rejected, not rendered; board shows a validation failure in chrome. | 1 |
| Position id not in vocabulary | `needs-attention`, labelled `unrecognised position: '<id>'`. **A discovery, not a bug.** | 1, 4 |
| Lane has no adapter | Position `dark` or `bagged`, `nominal` register. Not an alarm. | 3, 4 |

## 7. Test strategy

Red-first throughout. Rev 1 named two tests that could not be written as described; both
are replaced with deterministically-red equivalents.

- **Fixture corpus** - valid, malformed, missing, truncated, future-dated, unknown-position.
- **Law 4 test:** every lane in the registry appears in every snapshot, in every code path,
  including before the first poll. Fails red against a hand-constructed lanes object.
- **Law 1 test:** malformed state file yields a `failed` lane and **no partial data**.
- **`never-polled` test:** `/api/snapshot` before the first poll returns every lane as
  `never-polled` with `asOf: null`, and the view renders "not yet read" - never empty,
  never `dark`.
- **Detector test:** an unknown position id renders `unrecognised position: 'x'` verbatim
  and never coerces to a default.
- **Staleness test:** injected clock crosses `stalenessMs`; `fresh` becomes `stale`.
  Writable because §5.4 gives the assembler a stated interface.
- **Atomicity test (replaces rev 1's race test):** spy the fs module; assert the
  destination path is never passed to any write or truncate call and that exactly one
  `rename(tmp, dest)` occurs. An in-place implementation calls `writeFile(dest)` and never
  `rename`, so this fails for exactly its stated reason before the fix. Plus: assert
  `rename` over the destination succeeds **while a read handle on the destination is
  open**, run on Windows, because `EPERM`/`EBUSY` is the realistic failure of this strategy
  on this repo's primary platform - not a partial read. Any interleaving loop is a
  non-gating soak, never the red.
- **Single-definition test (replaces rev 1's vacuous one):** ESLint rule plus source scan;
  both red against a two-definition codebase.
- **Serialisation parity test:** `/api/snapshot` and `/api/stream` emit byte-identical
  payloads for a fixed snapshot.
- **Event-name test:** the server writes the constant the client subscribes to.
- **Ordering test:** a late-resolving first-paint fetch does not overwrite a newer streamed
  snapshot.
- **Heartbeat test:** two missed heartbeats flip the client to disconnected while
  last-good data remains displayed and marked.
- **Poll-overlap test:** a slow poll does not stack, and an older result never overwrites a
  newer snapshot.

## 8. Contracts instantiated

Both templates, not one. Rev 1 instantiated the ledger and silently dropped the gating
matrix - in the train that lands the matrix template's own worked example row.

**`docs/contracts/state-ledger.md`.** Lifecycle events, enumerated for StarCar:

1. Server restart
2. Adapter failure window (poll fails, then recovers)
3. Client reconnect
4. State file replaced or truncated (atomic rename swaps the inode under a live reader)
5. **Poll overlap** (a poll outlasting its interval)
6. **Pre-first-poll window** (server up, no result yet)

Fields: `lastGoodSnapshot`, `lastPollAt`, `pollInFlight`, `seq`, the timer handle,
`connectedClients`.

**`connectedClients` is ledgered.** The argument that it is "an implementation detail of
the SSE layer" is verbatim the argument that produced ninety-nine latent bugs in this
template's ancestor project. A ledger's first entry sets its threshold forever; this one is
set low, on purpose.

Browser-side state (rendered snapshot, connection status, last applied `seq`) is **in
scope** for the ledger, because "client reconnect" is a listed lifecycle event and
verdicting it against no client-side rows would be theatre.

**`docs/contracts/gating-matrix.md`.** Four gated surfaces land here: the staleness
banner, the disconnect indicator, the `failed` panel, and the unrecognised-position
detector.

## 9. Documentation ownership

Rev 1 ran a fourteen-dispatch train with **zero documentation scope**, in a repo whose
north star ranks documentation equal to code. Repaired:

- **Every car** updates every document its change invalidates, in the same commit. Its
  reviewer treats a stale document as a Major.
- **Car 5 owns `README.md` and the quickstart**, because it makes the thing runnable and
  `README.md:8-11` currently promises "no code, no server, and no quickstart" - a promise
  this train falsifies.
- **Car 1 owns `docs/setup.md`'s two firing triggers**: the GitNexus index row (trigger:
  first code lands) and the CI row (trigger: first workflow need). Both fire on this train.
- The quickstart is gated at the PR by sentence check, per `CLAUDE.md`, tracing prose →
  command → code → observed behaviour. CI's quickstart runner (#6) remains parked.

## 10. Cars

| Car | Scope | Why here |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Toolchain + CI + repo scaffolding; `docs/setup.md` trigger rows | **First, because "verified" means CI went green.** Until this lands, no later car can honestly claim verification. |
| 2 | `schema`, registries, position vocabulary, `StateFileAdapter`, `SnapshotAssembler` | Defines every contract the rest consume. Creates the state ledger. |
| 3 | `StateWriter` module + CLI | Depends on car 2's schema. |
| 4 | `Server`, poll loop, SSE, heartbeat, ordering; gating matrix | Depends on car 2. |
| 5 | `View`, register rendering, detector, chrome; README + quickstart | Depends on car 2 and car 4. |

Strictly ordered 1 → 2 → {3, 4} → 5. Each followed by its own adversarial reviewer.

**Toolchain (Car 1), stated because rev 1 omitted it entirely and it is the mechanism D2's
guarantee rides on:** Node 22 LTS; TypeScript compiled by `tsc` to native ES modules
served directly, no bundler, so the browser imports the same emitted module the server
imports and the type is physically shared rather than re-declared; one `tsconfig.json`;
vitest with the jsdom environment for view tests; ESLint for the single-definition rule.

**Configuration defaults** (Law 7 - a stranger must be able to run and tune this): host
`127.0.0.1`, port `4600`, `pollMs` 1000, `heartbeatMs` 5000, `stalenessMs` 15000, state
path resolved from CWD. Each overridable by flag and by `STARCAR_*` environment variable,
read once at startup. The effective values travel in `snapshot.config` and are rendered,
because a board that will not say what it is reading or how often is a Law 5 failure.

**Cost:** five cars and five reviewers, plus design, spec and plan with their reviews -
roughly 16 to 17 dispatches, up from rev 1's 14. The increase is one car (toolchain/CI)
and one REJECT round already spent. REJECT rounds are expected outcomes, not overruns.

**Tracking:** cars 1, 3 and 4 are not `area:view` work. Either they get their own issues
under `area:server`, `area:adapters` and `area:tooling`, or the conductor records a ruling
that this train runs against #1 alone. Flagged rather than assumed.

## 11. Explicitly out of scope

Git adapter. GitHub board adapter. Fuel/usage adapter. Auth. Persistence beyond the state
file. Any frontend framework. Theming. History or event log - `Yard` is a current-state
snapshot and a REJECT round is a counter, not an event stream. Multi-repo support.
Adapter-failure backoff (a local file poll at this scale does not need it; recorded so a
later reviewer knows it was considered).

**Law 2, explicitly.** "The Dispatcher Commands" is the second-ranked law and rev 1 neither
implemented nor deferred it. In v0 the dispatcher's override **is the StateWriter**: the
human edits the source of truth directly, and the board renders what the source says. A
view-level override - marking a train held from the browser, against the data - is
deliberately out of scope for v0 and is the natural second use of the writer.

**Lane-to-train linking.** The best idea this design produced is that a lane under
construction is a lane with a *train on it*, so the board could render its own build-out
using the machinery it renders everything else with. It is out of scope for v0: it couples
the lane registry to yard state, and we have no real train to link to yet. `under-construction`
ships as vocabulary now; the link comes when there is something to point at.

## 12. What changed from rev 1, and why

Rev 1 was REJECTed with nine Majors. The reviewer's citation audit found every
constitutional citation correct and the four-car split correctly sized; the defects were
in the contract and its completeness.

| Rev 1 finding | Disposition |
|---|---|
| MAJOR-1 no state for "server up, first poll pending" | **Dissolved** by §4.3 `never-polled`. Not a missing enum member - the enum was one axis where there were two. |
| MAJOR-2 sentence-check reduction unsound, shared-type test vacuous | **Fixed**, §5.2. Rev 1's central claim was wrong three ways; all three repaired with tests that can fail. |
| MAJOR-3 writer writes gates while signals declared adapter-less | **Dissolved** by `bagged`, §4.2. No feature cut. |
| MAJOR-4 SSE change detection, heartbeat, ordering | **Fixed**, §5.3, all three specified. |
| MAJOR-5 state inventory incomplete, `lastGood` ownership, ledger ownership | **Fixed**, §5.4 and §8. One copy of last-good, `pollInFlight` ledgered, ledger is car 2's deliverable and every later car appends. |
| MAJOR-6 `asOf` provenance unspecified | **Fixed**, §5.7, plus the fixture split from ruling Q2. |
| MAJOR-7 no documentation owner, gating matrix dropped, README goes false | **Fixed**, §8 and §9. |
| MAJOR-8 two unwritable tests | **Fixed**, §7, both replaced with deterministically-red equivalents, including the Windows rename-over-open-handle case rev 1 missed entirely. |
| MAJOR-9 no build/module/test-runner story | **Fixed**, §10. |
| Minor-1 wrong citation range | Fixed - the policy is at `board.ps1:9-14`, cited correctly in §5.6. |
| Minor-3 "zero inference means zero Law 1 risk" | Removed. It was false as a categorical: the assembler does derive, and MAJOR-1/4/6 were all Law 1 risks with no inference involved. |
| Minor-4 Law 2 absent | Addressed, §11. |
| Minor-6 Law 4 vs Law 7 trade unrecorded | Recorded, §4.5. |
| Minor-9 threshold never named | Named, §10. |
| Minor-10 car vocabulary not inherited | Now vocabulary, not enum - same open-set treatment as positions. |
| Minor-11 "smoke-tested" undefined | Defined, §5.6. |
| Minor-12 no host/port/URL | Defined, §10. |
| Minor-13/14 pre-existing stale docs | Swept in commit `e236252`, outside this design's scope. |
| Rulings Q1-Q5 | All adopted: §10 (interval), §5.7 + §10 (path and fixture split), §7 (one threshold, `ageMs` rendered), §8 (`connectedClients` ledgered), §5.5 (two truths, simultaneously displayable, disconnect in chrome). |

**The change rev 1's review did not ask for, and the reason for it:** §2. The reviewer
found a missing state; the owner then found three more and split one of those into three
again. The correct response was not a longer enum. It was to notice that a taxonomy which
grows on every observation should never have been compiled in - which Law 7 had already
ruled, before the question was asked.

## 13. Open questions for the design reviewer

1. Is `register` genuinely closed at three, or does `in-progress` collapse into `nominal`?
   A two-register board is simpler and Law 3 favours fewer distinctions.
2. Should `positions` travel on every snapshot, or be fetched once? Every snapshot is
   simpler and self-describing; it is also repeated bytes on every emit.
3. Is browser-side state genuinely ledger-worthy (§8), or is that the ledger over-reaching
   into a process it does not own?
4. `demoMode` suppresses the staleness alarm for a checked-in fixture. Is that a legitimate
   accommodation, or is it the first override of a truth surface and therefore a Law 1
   breach that should instead be solved by regenerating the fixture's `writtenAt`?
5. Car 1 (toolchain + CI) is ordered first so "verified" is achievable. Is that correct, or
   does CI on an empty repo test nothing and belong after car 2?

input.command:
cd /c/Users/Chris/git/starcar
git add -A
git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
design: v0 rev 2 - close what mechanism determines, open what the shop names

Rev 1 REJECTed with 9 Major / 14 Minor. All nine closed in the design (not
deferred to the spec - cars inherit these as ground truth), all five reviewer
rulings adopted, and the core contract re-cut.

The re-cut, which the review did not ask for and which is the real fix: rev 1
declared four lane states, its reviewer found a fifth, the owner then found
three more and split one of those into three again. That is not a sequence of
near-misses converging on a correct list - it is evidence the list does not
stabilise before observation. Running the yard is what generates the states.

Law 7 had already ruled on this and rev 1 disobeyed it: "no hardcoded board
schemas or label taxonomies". Rev 1 hardcoded a taxonomy and then spent three
rounds trying to get the hardcoded one right.

So: CLOSE what mechanism determines, OPEN what the shop names. Freshness is a
compile-time union because a poll has provably either never run, or last
succeeded, or last failed. Positions (live/bagged/dark/under-construction) are
DATA, carried on the wire so the view never hardcodes a vocabulary. Registers
stay closed at three because Law 3 needs a stable glance-language.

And the unrecognised-position path is not an error path, it is the DETECTOR:
the board renders "unrecognised position: 'x'" loudly and by name, which is
Law 1 and Law 4 doing their jobs AND the instrument that tells us we just
observed a state nobody enumerated. You do not resolve an observation-dependent
state space by guessing harder; you build the detector and open the box.

That dissolves MAJOR-1 (never-polled is a freshness state, not a missing enum
member) and MAJOR-3 (signals is 'bagged' - we hold gate data and say we do not
surface it) rather than patching them. No feature was cut to resolve either.

MAJOR-2 was rev 1's central error and is repaired three ways: a duplicate type
declaration is NOT a build failure in a structurally-typed language, so the
rev 1 "shared-type test" would have passed the moment it was written; JSON.parse
is an unchecked cast, so the view now runs the shared runtime validator on every
payload; and the SSE event name is a hand-written string on both sides that,
one character apart, leaves the socket OPEN while the client reports itself
connected forever. Event name is now a shared constant with a test.

Also: SSE heartbeat + ordering + change-detection specified (all three were
silent lies), poll-overlap guard, asOf from an in-file writtenAt rather than
mtime (git checkout rewrites mtime), future-dated timestamps rejected, live
state file split from the demo fixture, both contract templates instantiated
rather than one, documentation given owners, toolchain stated, and the two
unwritable tests replaced with deterministically-red equivalents including the
Windows rename-over-open-handle case rev 1 missed.

Cars go 4 -> 5, ordered, with toolchain+CI FIRST because "verified" means the
pipeline went green and until CI exists no car can honestly claim it.
Cost 14 -> 16-17 dispatches, stated before dispatch, not discovered on the bill.

Refs #1

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
git push -q origin dev
echo "=== committed ==="
git log --oneline -1
gh issue view 1 --repo polecatspeaks/StarCar --json body -q .body > /tmp/i1.md
cat >> /tmp/i1.md <<'EOF'


---

**AMENDMENT 2026-07-21 (owner-approved, recorded in the design doc §1 and §12).**

The ROUTE above is SUPERSEDED. It read "v0 static mock first to validate the layout, then the adapter/generator built through the full ladder." That was written before the delivery medium was decided.

**Replaced by:** a full-frame walking skeleton. All declared lanes present from the first commit, live server plus browser over SSE, trains fed by the conductor state file. There is no throwaway mock, because Law 4 forces honest non-live states to render loudly anyway - so the real layout appears in the real browser over the real transport at no extra cost.

The design also re-cuts the lane model itself: **close what mechanism determines, open what the shop names.** Freshness is a compile-time union; the position vocabulary (`live` / `bagged` / `dark` / `under-construction`) is DATA carried on the wire, per Law 7's ban on hardcoded label taxonomies. An unrecognised position renders loudly by name and is treated as a discovery, not a bug - the board is its own detector for states we have not observed yet.

Current design: `docs/design/2026-07-21-v0-yard-skeleton-design.md` (rev 2, in adversarial review). Rev 1 was REJECTed with 9 Major findings; the verdict and the re-cut are recorded in §12 of the design and in the commit history on `dev`.

*Body amended rather than only commented, because a comment does not un-say a body and a reader who scans and stops would take the superseded route as ground truth. Living-document rule, `CLAUDE.md` NORTH STAR.*
EOF
gh issue edit 1 --repo polecatspeaks/StarCar --body-file /tmp/i1.md > /dev/null
echo "=== issue #1 body amended ==="
